questions.stem: A claim submitted without a valid ICD-10-CM diagnosis code will still be processed for payment as long as a procedure code is present.

questions.stem: Standardized transaction formats allow any provider to submit a claim to any health plan using the same data structure, enabling interoperability across the healthcare system.

questions.stem: In the basic claims flow, the provider submits a claim to the payor after the patient receives care.

questions.stem: Providers may submit claims to payors using any proprietary format they choose, as long as the claim data is accurate.

questions.stem: Automated edits performed on incoming claims check for completeness, valid values, duplicate submissions, and beneficiary eligibility.

questions.stem: A rejected claim and a denied claim mean the same thing in healthcare billing.

questions.stem: A payor will approve a claim as long as a valid procedure code is present, regardless of the diagnosis code submitted.
questions.feedback: Payors use the diagnosis code to determine medical necessity. If the diagnosis does not support the procedure according to coverage policy, the claim may be denied.

questions.stem: Patients should compare the Explanation of Benefits (EOB) they receive from their health plan to the provider's actual bill to check for accuracy.
questions.feedback: An EOB is a summary of how the plan processed the claim, and patients should compare it to the provider's bill to verify the amounts are correct.

questions.stem: The Electronic Remittance Advice (ERA) is sent from the provider to the payor to explain why a claim was submitted.
questions.feedback: The ERA (HIPAA 835 transaction) is sent from the payor to the provider, not the other way around. It details payment amounts, adjustments, and denial reasons.

questions.stem: For in-network services, a provider generally cannot bill the patient beyond the allowed amount set by the health plan.
questions.feedback: In-network providers have agreed to accept the health plan's allowed amount as the maximum charge. The patient may owe cost-sharing (co-pay, coinsurance, deductible) but not charges above the allowed amount.

questions.stem: A provider's NPI number changes whenever the provider moves to a new state or changes specialties.
questions.feedback: The NPI is permanent and does not change based on location, specialty, or any other provider attribute. It is a permanent, intelligence-free identifier.

questions.stem: Protected health information (PHI) includes any data that relates to a person's health condition, care received, or payment for care, and that can identify the individual.
questions.feedback: PHI is defined as individually identifiable health information that relates to a person's health condition, care received, or payment for that care.

questions.stem: Under HIPAA, a covered entity must always obtain written patient authorization before sharing PHI for treatment purposes.
questions.feedback: Under HIPAA, covered entities may share PHI without patient authorization for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO).

questions.stem: Under HIPAA, patients have the right to access and obtain copies of their own health records from a covered entity.
questions.feedback: HIPAA gives patients the right to access and obtain copies of their health records from any covered entity within 30 days of request.

questions.stem: A clearinghouse sends claims directly to patients for review before forwarding them to the health plan.
questions.feedback: A clearinghouse is an intermediary between providers and health plans. It converts transactions into HIPAA-standard formats and forwards them to the health plan — patients are not part of this process.

questions.stem: ASC X12 Version 5010 is the current HIPAA-adopted standard format for electronic healthcare transactions.
questions.feedback: ASC X12 Version 5010 is the HIPAA-adopted standard used by providers, payors, and clearinghouses nationwide for electronic transactions.

questions.stem: HIPAA mandates standard electronic formats for claims submission but does not standardize eligibility verification or remittance advice transactions.
questions.feedback: HIPAA's Administrative Simplification provisions standardize multiple transaction types including claims (837), remittance advice (835), eligibility verification (270/271), claim status (276/277), and referral authorization (278).

questions.stem: Providers can verify a patient's insurance eligibility before providing care by sending an electronic eligibility inquiry to the health plan.
questions.feedback: Providers can send a HIPAA 270 transaction to the health plan before or at the time of service to verify coverage and benefits.

questions.stem: Each health plan requires a unique, plan-specific data format for electronic claims, making it impossible for one provider to submit to multiple plans using the same format.
questions.feedback: Standardized transaction formats allow any provider to submit a claim to any health plan using the same data structure, enabling interoperability across the healthcare system.

questions.stem: An Explanation of Benefits (EOB) shows provider charges, allowed charges, the amount the insurer paid, and what the patient owes.
questions.feedback: The EOB is a document sent by the health plan to the patient after a claim is processed, showing all of these amounts.

questions.stem: Who initiates a claim in the basic healthcare claims flow?
questions.feedback: In the standard claims flow, the provider submits a claim to the payor after the patient receives care.

options.label: A
options.text: The patient submits the claim to the health plan
options.feedback: Patients do not typically submit claims; the provider handles claim submission.

options.label: B
options.text: The provider submits the claim to the payor after delivering care
options.feedback: In the standard claims flow, the provider submits a claim to the payor after the patient receives care.

options.label: C
options.text: The payor generates the claim automatically when care is delivered
options.feedback: Payors do not automatically generate claims; they receive and process claims submitted by providers.

options.label: D
options.text: The clearinghouse creates the claim on behalf of the patient
options.feedback: Clearinghouses translate and forward claims but do not create them on behalf of patients.

questions.stem: What method do providers primarily use to submit claims to payors under HIPAA?
questions.feedback: HIPAA requires providers to submit claims electronically using standard transaction formats.

options.label: A
options.text: Paper forms mailed through the postal service
options.feedback: While paper claims still exist in limited cases, HIPAA promotes and often requires electronic submission.

options.label: B
options.text: Electronic submission using HIPAA-standard transaction formats
options.feedback: HIPAA requires providers to submit claims electronically using standard transaction formats.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Claims are not submitted verbally.

options.label: D
options.text: Faxed copies of the patient's medical record
options.feedback: Faxing medical records is not the standard method of claim submission under HIPAA.

questions.stem: Which of the following is checked during the automated edits performed on an incoming claim?
questions.feedback: Automated edits check for completeness, valid values, data consistency, duplicate submissions, and provider/beneficiary eligibility.

options.label: A
options.feedback: A provider's credit score is not part of claim processing.

options.label: B
options.text: Whether the patient has paid all previous medical bills
options.feedback: The patient's prior bill payment history is not checked during automated claim edits.

options.label: C
options.text: Completeness of claim data, valid values, and duplicate submissions
options.feedback: Automated edits check for completeness, valid values, data consistency, duplicate submissions, and provider/beneficiary eligibility.

options.label: D
options.text: Whether the patient's employer has approved the visit
options.feedback: Employer approval is not part of the automated claim edit process.

questions.stem: What happens to a claim before it reaches the adjudication stage?
questions.feedback: Before adjudication, automated edits verify completeness, valid values, data consistency, duplicate status, and beneficiary eligibility.

options.label: A
options.text: It is sent to the patient for approval
options.feedback: Patients do not approve claims before adjudication.

options.label: B
options.text: It undergoes automated edits that check for completeness and eligibility
options.feedback: Before adjudication, automated edits verify completeness, valid values, data consistency, duplicate status, and beneficiary eligibility.

options.label: C
options.text: It is published in a public database for transparency
options.feedback: Claims are not published in any public database.

options.label: D
options.text: It is forwarded to a government agency for pre-approval
options.feedback: Claims are not routinely forwarded to a government agency for pre-approval.

questions.stem: What does it mean when a healthcare claim is "rejected"?
questions.feedback: A rejected claim is returned to the provider for correction, typically because of errors or missing information, and can be resubmitted.

options.label: A
options.text: The payor has determined the service is not covered under the patient's plan
options.feedback: This describes a denied claim, not a rejected one.

options.label: B
options.text: The claim is returned to the provider for correction and may be resubmitted
options.feedback: A rejected claim is returned to the provider for correction, typically because of errors or missing information, and can be resubmitted.

options.label: C
options.text: The patient must appeal the decision to a government agency
options.feedback: A rejected claim does not require a government appeal; it requires correction and resubmission.

options.label: D
options.text: The provider must refund the patient in full
options.feedback: Rejection does not require a refund; the provider corrects and resubmits the claim.

questions.stem: What is one reason a claim might be denied after adjudication?
questions.feedback: A claim may be denied if the service is not covered or does not meet policy requirements.

options.label: A
options.text: The claim form was submitted using the correct HIPAA format
options.feedback: Using the correct format supports claim processing, not denial.

options.label: B
options.text: The service is not covered under the patient's health plan
options.feedback: A claim may be denied if the service is not covered or does not meet policy requirements.

options.label: C
options.text: The provider included a valid NPI on the claim
options.feedback: Including a valid NPI is required and would not cause a denial.

options.label: D
options.text: The patient's eligibility was verified before the visit
options.feedback: Verified eligibility supports processing, not denial.

questions.stem: When can a provider send an eligibility inquiry (270 transaction) to a health plan?
questions.feedback: Providers can verify eligibility before or at the time of service by sending a HIPAA 270 transaction to the health plan.

options.label: A
options.text: Only after the claim has been denied
options.feedback: Eligibility verification is typically done before or at the time of service, not only after a denial.

options.label: B
options.text: Only during the annual open enrollment period
options.feedback: Eligibility inquiries can be sent at any time, not just during open enrollment.

options.label: C
options.text: Before or at the time of service
options.feedback: Providers can verify eligibility before or at the time of service by sending a HIPAA 270 transaction to the health plan.

options.label: D
options.text: Only after the patient has been discharged from the hospital
options.feedback: Checking eligibility after discharge would be too late for most purposes.

questions.stem: What type of information is included in a real-time eligibility response from a health plan?
questions.feedback: Since 2013, HIPAA operating rules require real-time responses that include deductible, co-pay, and coinsurance information.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Tax return information is not part of eligibility responses.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Since 2013, HIPAA operating rules require real-time responses that include deductible, co-pay, and coinsurance information.

options.label: C
options.text: A list of all claims the patient has ever filed
options.feedback: A full claims history is not returned in an eligibility response.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Medical history is not part of an eligibility verification response.

questions.stem: Why must every healthcare claim pair a diagnosis code with a procedure code?
questions.feedback: The diagnosis code (ICD-10-CM) explains why care was needed, and the procedure code (CPT/HCPCS) describes the service performed; together they link clinical documentation to reimbursement.

options.label: A
options.text: To satisfy the provider's internal record-keeping requirements only
options.feedback: While codes appear in records, the pairing requirement is driven by billing and reimbursement, not just internal record-keeping.

options.label: B
options.text: To link the clinical reason for care to the service performed, supporting the payor's reimbursement decision
options.feedback: The diagnosis code (ICD-10-CM) explains why care was needed, and the procedure code (CPT/HCPCS) describes the service performed; together they link clinical documentation to reimbursement.

options.label: C
options.text: To allow the patient to choose their preferred treatment option
options.feedback: Code pairing is about billing, not patient treatment choice.

options.label: D
options.text: To ensure the clearinghouse can assign an NPI to the claim
options.feedback: The clearinghouse does not assign NPIs based on code pairing.

questions.stem: What happens when a provider submits a claim without a valid ICD-10-CM diagnosis code?
questions.feedback: A claim without a valid ICD-10-CM diagnosis code is returned as incomplete and will not be processed for payment.

options.label: A
options.text: The payor processes the claim using a default diagnosis code
options.feedback: Payors do not assign default diagnosis codes to incomplete claims.

options.label: B
options.text: The claim is returned to the provider as incomplete and will not be processed
options.feedback: A claim without a valid ICD-10-CM diagnosis code is returned as incomplete and will not be processed for payment.

options.label: C
options.text: The clearinghouse assigns a diagnosis code automatically
options.feedback: Clearinghouses translate formats but do not add clinical codes to claims.

options.label: D
options.text: The patient is contacted to provide the diagnosis information
options.feedback: Patients are not responsible for providing diagnosis codes.

questions.stem: How do payors use the diagnosis code on a claim?
questions.feedback: Payors use the diagnosis code to determine medical necessity — whether the diagnosis supports the procedure according to coverage policy.

options.label: A
options.text: To assign the patient a primary care physician
options.feedback: Diagnosis codes are not used to assign physicians.

options.label: B
options.text: To determine whether the procedure is medically necessary for the reported condition
options.feedback: Payors use the diagnosis code to determine medical necessity — whether the diagnosis supports the procedure according to coverage policy.

options.label: C
options.text: To calculate the provider's annual income
options.feedback: Diagnosis codes do not relate to provider income calculations.

options.label: D
options.text: To schedule the patient's next appointment
options.feedback: Scheduling is not a function of diagnosis codes on claims.

questions.stem: Who receives the Explanation of Benefits (EOB)?
questions.feedback: The EOB is sent by the health plan to the patient after a claim is processed.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Clearinghouses do not receive EOBs.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Providers receive the ERA (835), not the EOB. The EOB goes to the patient.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The EOB is sent by the health plan to the patient after a claim is processed.

options.label: D
options.feedback: EOBs go to the patient, not the employer.

questions.stem: Which of the following pieces of information appears on an Explanation of Benefits (EOB)?
questions.feedback: An EOB shows provider charges, allowed charges, the amount the insurer paid, and what the patient owes.

options.label: A
options.feedback: NPI application information does not appear on an EOB.

options.label: B
options.text: The allowed charges and the amount the insurer paid
options.feedback: An EOB shows provider charges, allowed charges, the amount the insurer paid, and what the patient owes.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Prescription history is not included on an EOB for a specific claim.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Clearinghouse fees are not shown on a patient's EOB.

questions.stem: Who sends the Electronic Remittance Advice (ERA) and who receives it?
questions.feedback: After adjudication, the payor sends an ERA (HIPAA 835 transaction) to the provider detailing payment, adjustments, and denial reasons.

options.label: A
options.text: The patient sends it to the provider
options.feedback: Patients are not involved in sending ERAs.

options.label: B
options.text: The provider sends it to the payor
options.feedback: The provider submits claims, not ERAs. The ERA flows in the opposite direction.

options.label: C
options.text: The payor sends it to the provider
options.feedback: After adjudication, the payor sends an ERA (HIPAA 835 transaction) to the provider detailing payment, adjustments, and denial reasons.

options.label: D
options.text: The clearinghouse sends it to the patient
options.feedback: The ERA goes to the provider, not the patient.

questions.stem: What information does an Electronic Remittance Advice (ERA) include?
questions.feedback: The ERA details the payment amount, adjustments, and reasons for any denials after claim adjudication.

options.label: A
options.text: The patient's treatment plan and medication list
options.feedback: Treatment plans and medication lists are clinical documents, not part of the ERA.

options.label: B
options.text: Payment amount, adjustments, and reasons for any denials
options.feedback: The ERA details the payment amount, adjustments, and reasons for any denials after claim adjudication.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Malpractice insurance details are not included in an ERA.

options.label: D
options.text: A referral to a specialist for follow-up care
options.feedback: Specialist referrals are not part of the ERA.

questions.stem: What is the "allowed amount" in healthcare reimbursement?
questions.feedback: The allowed amount is the maximum the health plan will pay for a covered service. The provider may bill the patient for cost-sharing but generally not above the allowed amount for in-network services.

options.label: A
options.text: The total charge the provider bills before insurance processing
options.feedback: The total billed charge is different from the allowed amount; the billed amount is often higher.

options.label: B
options.text: The maximum amount a health plan will pay for a covered service
options.feedback: The allowed amount is the maximum the health plan will pay for a covered service. The provider may bill the patient for cost-sharing but generally not above the allowed amount for in-network services.

options.label: C
options.text: The amount the patient pays as a monthly premium
options.feedback: Monthly premiums are separate from the allowed amount for individual services.

options.label: D
options.text: The amount charged by the clearinghouse for processing the claim
options.feedback: Clearinghouse fees are not related to the allowed amount.

questions.stem: After the health plan pays its share of the allowed amount, what may the patient still owe for an in-network service?
questions.feedback: The patient may owe cost-sharing amounts such as co-pay, coinsurance, or deductible, but not charges above the allowed amount for in-network services.

options.label: A
options.text: The full billed charge from the provider
options.feedback: For in-network services, the patient's responsibility is limited to cost-sharing within the allowed amount, not the full billed charge.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The patient may owe cost-sharing amounts such as co-pay, coinsurance, or deductible, but not charges above the allowed amount for in-network services.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Patients do not pay clearinghouse fees.

options.label: D
options.text: A penalty for using an in-network provider
options.feedback: There is no penalty for using in-network providers; in fact, costs are typically lower in-network.

questions.stem: What does a healthcare clearinghouse do with claims it receives from providers?
questions.feedback: A clearinghouse receives claims from providers, converts them into HIPAA-standard formats, and forwards them to the appropriate health plan.

options.label: A
options.text: Diagnoses the patient's condition and updates the claim
options.feedback: Clearinghouses handle data formatting, not clinical diagnosis.

options.label: B
options.text: Converts the claims into HIPAA-standard electronic formats and forwards them to the health plan
options.feedback: A clearinghouse receives claims from providers, converts them into HIPAA-standard formats, and forwards them to the appropriate health plan.

options.label: C
options.text: Stores the claims permanently and does not forward them
options.feedback: Clearinghouses forward claims to health plans; they do not permanently hold them.

options.label: D
options.text: Returns the claims to the patient for verification
options.feedback: Claims are forwarded to health plans, not returned to patients.

questions.stem: Why might a provider use a clearinghouse instead of submitting claims directly to each health plan?
questions.feedback: Clearinghouses serve as intermediaries that translate and format claims into HIPAA-standard formats, making it easier for providers to submit to multiple health plans.

options.label: A
options.text: Clearinghouses provide medical care that the provider cannot
options.feedback: Clearinghouses do not provide medical care.

options.label: B
options.text: Clearinghouses convert claims into the correct standard format for each plan, simplifying submission
options.feedback: Clearinghouses serve as intermediaries that translate and format claims into HIPAA-standard formats, making it easier for providers to submit to multiple health plans.

options.label: C
options.text: Clearinghouses are required to approve all claims before payors can process them
options.feedback: Clearinghouses translate formats but do not approve claims.

options.label: D
options.text: Clearinghouses eliminate the need for diagnosis and procedure codes
options.feedback: Clearinghouses do not remove the requirement for clinical codes.

questions.stem: Which of the following are the three types of HIPAA covered entities?
questions.feedback: The three HIPAA covered entity types are health plans, health care providers who transmit transactions electronically, and clearinghouses.

options.label: A
options.feedback: These are specific types of providers, not the three covered entity categories.

options.label: B
options.text: Health plans, health care providers who transmit transactions electronically, and clearinghouses
options.feedback: The three HIPAA covered entity types are health plans, health care providers who transmit transactions electronically, and clearinghouses.

options.label: C
options.feedback: These are types of health plans, not the three covered entity categories.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Patients, employers, and government agencies are not the three covered entity types under HIPAA.

questions.stem: What does it mean that the NPI is "intelligence-free"?
questions.feedback: The NPI is intelligence-free, meaning the number itself carries no information about the provider's location, specialty, or type — it is purely a unique identifier.

options.label: A
options.text: It does not require a computer to look up
options.feedback: Intelligence-free describes the number carrying no embedded meaning, not whether a computer is needed to look it up.

options.label: B
options.text: It carries no information about the provider's specialty, location, or type
options.feedback: The NPI is intelligence-free, meaning the number itself carries no information about the provider's location, specialty, or type — it is purely a unique identifier.

options.label: C
options.text: It is automatically generated without human review
options.feedback: Intelligence-free refers to the number format carrying no provider information, not to how the number is reviewed or generated.

options.label: D
options.text: It expires and must be renewed every 10 years
options.feedback: The NPI does not expire or require renewal; intelligence-free refers to the number carrying no embedded information.

questions.stem: On which types of transactions must the NPI appear?
questions.feedback: The NPI must appear on all electronic claims, eligibility inquiries, and other HIPAA transactions.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The NPI is required on electronic HIPAA transactions, not limited to paper claims.

options.label: B
options.text: Only on claims for Medicare patients
options.feedback: The NPI is required for all HIPAA-covered transactions, not only Medicare.

options.label: C
options.text: All electronic claims, eligibility inquiries, and other HIPAA transactions
options.feedback: The NPI must appear on all electronic claims, eligibility inquiries, and other HIPAA transactions.

options.label: D
options.text: Only on transactions processed by clearinghouses
options.feedback: The NPI requirement applies to all HIPAA transactions, not just those routed through clearinghouses.

questions.stem: What role does a healthcare clearinghouse play in the claims process?
questions.feedback: A healthcare clearinghouse receives claims and other transactions from providers, converts them from non-standard formats into the standardized electronic formats required by HIPAA, and forwards them to the appropriate payor.

options.label: A
options.text: It directly pays providers for services rendered
options.feedback: Clearinghouses do not pay providers; payors (health plans) are responsible for reimbursement.

options.label: B
options.text: It converts non-standard claim data into standard HIPAA formats for submission to payors
options.feedback: A healthcare clearinghouse receives claims and other transactions from providers, converts them from non-standard formats into the standardized electronic formats required by HIPAA, and forwards them to the appropriate payor.

options.label: C
options.text: It determines whether a patient is eligible for coverage
options.feedback: Eligibility determination is performed by the health plan, not the clearinghouse.

options.label: D
options.text: It assigns diagnosis and procedure codes to patient encounters
options.feedback: Coding is performed by the provider or medical coder, not by the clearinghouse.

questions.stem: Which of the following entities is NOT a HIPAA covered entity?
questions.feedback: HIPAA covered entities are health plans, health care providers who transmit transactions electronically, and clearinghouses. A life insurance company that does not handle health information is not a covered entity.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Health insurance companies are health plans and are covered entities under HIPAA.

options.label: B
options.text: A hospital that submits claims electronically
options.feedback: Hospitals that transmit electronic transactions are covered entities.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Clearinghouses are one of the three types of covered entities.

options.label: D
options.text: A life insurance company that does not handle health information
options.feedback: HIPAA covered entities are health plans, health care providers who transmit transactions electronically, and clearinghouses. A life insurance company that does not handle health information is not a covered entity.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of protected health information (PHI)?
questions.feedback: PHI is any data relating to a person's health condition, care, or payment for care that can identify the individual — such as a name linked to a diagnosis.

options.label: A
options.text: A patient's name linked to their diagnosis in a medical record
options.feedback: PHI is any data relating to a person's health condition, care, or payment for care that can identify the individual — such as a name linked to a diagnosis.

options.label: B
options.text: Aggregate hospital admission statistics with no individual identifiers
options.feedback: Aggregate data without individual identifiers is not PHI.

options.label: C
options.text: A provider's office address published on their website
options.feedback: A provider's public business address is not PHI.

options.label: D
options.text: The total revenue of a health plan reported in annual filings
options.feedback: A health plan's revenue figures are not individually identifiable health information.

questions.stem: Under HIPAA, which set of purposes allows a covered entity to share PHI without patient authorization?
questions.feedback: HIPAA allows covered entities to share PHI without patient authorization for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO).

options.label: A
options.feedback: Marketing and fundraising generally require patient authorization under HIPAA.

options.label: B
options.text: Treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO)
options.feedback: HIPAA allows covered entities to share PHI without patient authorization for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO).

options.label: C
options.text: Employment, insurance underwriting, and law enforcement
options.feedback: These are not the TPO purposes recognized by HIPAA for sharing PHI without authorization.

options.label: D
options.text: Education, media reporting, and public relations
options.feedback: Education and media reporting are not TPO purposes under HIPAA.

questions.stem: What does HIPAA's minimum necessary standard require?
questions.feedback: The minimum necessary standard requires covered entities to limit PHI use and disclosure to the smallest amount needed to accomplish the intended purpose.

options.label: A
options.text: That covered entities use the minimum number of staff to handle PHI
options.feedback: The standard applies to information disclosed, not staffing levels.

options.label: B
options.text: That PHI disclosures be limited to the smallest amount of information needed for the purpose
options.feedback: The minimum necessary standard requires covered entities to limit PHI use and disclosure to the smallest amount needed to accomplish the intended purpose.

options.label: C
options.text: That patients provide the minimum amount of personal information at check-in
options.feedback: The standard governs how covered entities handle PHI, not what patients provide at check-in.

options.label: D
options.text: That providers use the fewest possible procedure codes on a claim
options.feedback: The standard is about PHI disclosure, not the number of procedure codes on a claim.

questions.stem: A hospital sends a patient's complete medical record to an insurance company that only requested lab results for a specific claim. Which HIPAA principle does this violate?
questions.feedback: Sending the complete medical record when only lab results were needed violates the minimum necessary standard, which requires limiting PHI to the smallest amount needed.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The right of access applies to patients requesting their own records.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Sending the complete medical record when only lab results were needed violates the minimum necessary standard, which requires limiting PHI to the smallest amount needed.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The breach notification rule covers unauthorized disclosures; this is a minimum necessary violation.

options.label: D
options.feedback: TPO is a category of permitted uses, not a principle about limiting the amount of information shared.

questions.stem: Within how many days must a covered entity provide a patient with copies of their health records after receiving a request?
questions.feedback: Under HIPAA, covered entities must provide patients with access to their health records within 30 days of request.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The HIPAA requirement is 30 days, not 7.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The HIPAA requirement is 30 days, not 14.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Under HIPAA, covered entities must provide patients with access to their health records within 30 days of request.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The HIPAA requirement is 30 days, not 90.

questions.stem: Under HIPAA, patients have the right to do which of the following with their health records?
questions.feedback: Patients have the right under HIPAA to access and obtain copies of their health records and to request corrections.

options.label: A
options.text: Delete any records they disagree with
options.feedback: Patients can request corrections, not unilateral deletions.

options.label: B
options.text: Access their records and request corrections
options.feedback: Patients have the right under HIPAA to access and obtain copies of their health records and to request corrections.

options.label: C
options.text: Sell their records to third-party researchers
options.feedback: HIPAA provides access rights, not the right to sell records.

options.label: D
options.text: Prevent their provider from documenting any care
options.feedback: Patients cannot prevent providers from maintaining required clinical documentation.

questions.stem: Which HIPAA standard transaction is used for claims submission?
questions.feedback: The 837 transaction is the HIPAA standard for electronic claims submission.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The 835 is the remittance advice (payment) transaction.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The 837 transaction is the HIPAA standard for electronic claims submission.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The 270 is the eligibility inquiry transaction.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The 278 is the referral authorization transaction.

questions.stem: Which HIPAA standard transaction is used for remittance advice (payment details)?
questions.feedback: The 835 transaction is the HIPAA standard for electronic remittance advice, which details payment information from the payor to the provider.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The 837 is used for claims submission, not remittance advice.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The 270 is used for eligibility inquiries.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The 835 transaction is the HIPAA standard for electronic remittance advice, which details payment information from the payor to the provider.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The 276 is used for claim status inquiries.

questions.stem: Which pair of HIPAA transactions covers eligibility verification?
questions.feedback: The 270 transaction is the eligibility inquiry, and the 271 is the eligibility response.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The 837 is for claims submission and the 835 is for remittance advice.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The 270 transaction is the eligibility inquiry, and the 271 is the eligibility response.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The 276/277 pair covers claim status inquiries and responses.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The 278 is for referral authorization; there is no standard 279 transaction.

questions.stem: Which HIPAA transaction pair is used for claim status inquiries and responses?
questions.feedback: The 276 transaction is the claim status inquiry, and the 277 is the claim status response.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The 270/271 pair is used for eligibility verification, not claim status.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The 837 is for claims submission and the 835 is for remittance advice.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The 276 transaction is the claim status inquiry, and the 277 is the claim status response.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The 278 is for referral authorization, and the 835 is for remittance advice; they are not a paired transaction.

questions.stem: What is the HIPAA 278 transaction used for?
questions.feedback: The 278 transaction is the HIPAA standard for referral authorization requests and responses.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Claims submission uses the 837 transaction.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Remittance advice uses the 835 transaction.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The 278 transaction is the HIPAA standard for referral authorization requests and responses.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Eligibility verification uses the 270/271 transaction pair.

questions.stem: Which organizations use the ASC X12 Version 5010 standard?
questions.feedback: ASC X12 Version 5010 is used by providers, payors, and clearinghouses nationwide as the HIPAA-adopted standard for electronic transactions.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The standard applies to all HIPAA-covered entities, not just Medicare and Medicaid.

options.label: B
options.feedback: ASC X12 Version 5010 is used by providers, payors, and clearinghouses nationwide as the HIPAA-adopted standard for electronic transactions.

options.label: C
options.text: Only hospitals with more than 100 beds
options.feedback: The standard applies to all covered providers, regardless of size.

options.label: D
options.text: Only clearinghouses that process government claims
options.feedback: All clearinghouses use this standard, not just those processing government claims.

questions.stem: What is the primary benefit of standardized electronic transaction formats in healthcare?
questions.feedback: Standardized formats enable interoperability — any provider can communicate with any health plan using the same data structure.

options.label: A
options.text: They allow patients to submit their own claims directly to the government
options.feedback: Patients typically do not submit claims; providers do.

options.label: B
options.text: They enable any provider to submit a claim to any health plan using the same data structure
options.feedback: Standardized formats enable interoperability — any provider can communicate with any health plan using the same data structure.

options.label: C
options.text: They eliminate the need for diagnosis and procedure codes
options.feedback: Standardized formats still require diagnosis and procedure codes.

options.label: D
options.text: They allow payors to set their own unique claim formats
options.feedback: The purpose of standardization is a common format, not plan-specific ones.

questions.stem: In the basic claims flow, what is the payor's role?
questions.feedback: In the claims flow, the payor (health plan) receives the claim, processes it through adjudication, and issues payment.

options.label: A
options.text: To deliver care to the patient
options.feedback: Delivering care is the provider's role.

options.label: B
options.text: To convert claims into standard formats
options.feedback: Converting claims to standard formats is the clearinghouse's role.

options.label: C
options.text: To process the claim and issue payment to the provider
options.feedback: In the claims flow, the payor (health plan) receives the claim, processes it through adjudication, and issues payment.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Scheduling appointments is not the payor's role in the claims flow.

questions.stem: Which coding system is used for diagnosis codes on a healthcare claim?
questions.feedback: ICD-10-CM is the coding system used for diagnosis codes, describing the clinical reason care was needed.

options.label: A
options.feedback: CPT codes describe procedures and services, not diagnoses.

options.label: B
options.feedback: HCPCS Level II codes describe supplies and services, not diagnoses.

options.label: C
options.feedback: ICD-10-CM is the coding system used for diagnosis codes, describing the clinical reason care was needed.

options.label: D
options.feedback: ASC X12 is a transaction standard format, not a coding system for diagnoses.

questions.stem: Which coding systems are used for procedure codes on healthcare claims?
questions.feedback: CPT and HCPCS codes describe the procedures and services performed, and are paired with ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes on claims.

options.label: A
options.feedback: ICD-10-PCS is used for inpatient procedure coding; CPT and HCPCS are the standard procedure code sets on claims.

options.label: B
options.feedback: CPT and HCPCS codes describe the procedures and services performed, and are paired with ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes on claims.

options.label: C
options.feedback: NPI is a provider identifier and EIN is a tax number; neither is a procedure code system.

options.label: D
options.feedback: These are HIPAA transaction numbers, not coding systems.

questions.stem: If a provider submits a claim with a diagnosis code that does not support the procedure code according to the payor's coverage policy, what is the likely result?
questions.feedback: If the diagnosis does not support the procedure according to coverage policy, the payor may deny the claim for lack of medical necessity.

options.label: A
options.text: The claim is approved at a higher rate
options.feedback: A mismatched diagnosis-procedure pair would not increase payment.

options.label: B
options.text: The claim may be denied for lack of medical necessity
options.feedback: If the diagnosis does not support the procedure according to coverage policy, the payor may deny the claim for lack of medical necessity.

options.label: C
options.text: The payor automatically changes the diagnosis code
options.feedback: Payors do not automatically change diagnosis codes submitted by providers.

options.label: D
options.text: The clearinghouse removes the procedure code before forwarding
options.feedback: Clearinghouses do not alter clinical codes on claims.

questions.stem: Why is it important for patients to understand that an EOB is not a bill?
questions.feedback: An EOB is a summary of how the plan processed the claim, showing charges, allowed amounts, plan payments, and patient responsibility. Patients should compare it to the actual bill from the provider.

options.label: A
options.text: Because the EOB shows more than the patient owes — it includes how the plan processed the entire claim
options.feedback: An EOB is a summary of how the plan processed the claim, showing charges, allowed amounts, plan payments, and patient responsibility. Patients should compare it to the actual bill from the provider.

options.label: B
options.text: Because the EOB requires immediate payment to the health plan
options.feedback: The EOB does not require payment to the health plan; the patient pays the provider.

options.label: C
options.text: Because the EOB replaces the provider's bill entirely
options.feedback: The EOB does not replace the provider's bill; patients should compare both documents.

options.label: D
options.text: Because the EOB can be used as a tax deduction receipt
options.feedback: An EOB is not designed as a tax receipt.

questions.stem: What changed about eligibility verification on January 1, 2013?
questions.feedback: HIPAA operating rules effective January 1, 2013, require health plans to respond to eligibility inquiries in real time, including deductible, co-pay, and coinsurance information.

options.label: A
options.text: Eligibility verification was no longer required for Medicare patients
options.feedback: Eligibility verification is still required for Medicare.

options.label: B
options.text: HIPAA operating rules began requiring health plans to respond to eligibility inquiries in real time
options.feedback: HIPAA operating rules effective January 1, 2013, require health plans to respond to eligibility inquiries in real time, including deductible, co-pay, and coinsurance information.

options.label: C
options.text: Providers were no longer allowed to verify eligibility before providing care
options.feedback: Providers are still permitted and encouraged to verify eligibility before providing care.

options.label: D
options.text: The 270 transaction was replaced by a new standard
options.feedback: The 270 transaction is still the standard for eligibility inquiries.

questions.stem: A healthcare clearinghouse handles PHI when processing claims. Under HIPAA, what obligation does this create?
questions.feedback: Clearinghouses are one of the three types of HIPAA covered entities and must protect PHI under the Privacy Rule.

options.label: A
options.text: The clearinghouse must delete all PHI within 24 hours
options.feedback: There is no 24-hour deletion requirement; the obligation is to protect PHI.

options.label: B
options.text: The clearinghouse must protect PHI as a HIPAA covered entity
options.feedback: Clearinghouses are one of the three types of HIPAA covered entities and must protect PHI under the Privacy Rule.

options.label: C
options.text: The clearinghouse is exempt from HIPAA because it does not provide care
options.feedback: Clearinghouses are explicitly included as HIPAA covered entities.

options.label: D
options.text: The clearinghouse must obtain patient authorization before forwarding each claim
options.feedback: Clearinghouses process claims under TPO and do not need individual patient authorization for each claim.

questions.stem: A provider shares a patient's lab results with a specialist to coordinate treatment. Does this require the patient's written authorization under HIPAA?
questions.feedback: Under HIPAA, covered entities may share PHI for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO) without patient authorization. Coordinating care with a specialist falls under treatment.

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, written authorization is always required before sharing any PHI
options.feedback: HIPAA does not require written authorization for TPO purposes.

options.label: B
options.text: No, sharing PHI for treatment is permitted under the TPO exception without patient authorization
options.feedback: Under HIPAA, covered entities may share PHI for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO) without patient authorization. Coordinating care with a specialist falls under treatment.

options.label: C
options.text: Yes, but only if the specialist is out of network
options.feedback: The TPO exception applies regardless of network status.

options.label: D
options.text: No, because lab results are not considered PHI
options.feedback: Lab results linked to a patient are PHI.

questions.stem: HIPAA's Administrative Simplification provisions mandate standard formats for which of the following?
questions.feedback: HIPAA's Administrative Simplification provisions mandate standard electronic formats for all of these key transaction types.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The provisions cover multiple transaction types, not just claims submission.

options.label: B
options.text: Claims (837), remittance (835), eligibility (270/271), claim status (276/277), and referral authorization (278)
options.feedback: HIPAA's Administrative Simplification provisions mandate standard electronic formats for all of these key transaction types.

options.label: C
options.text: Only eligibility verification (270/271) and remittance advice (835)
options.feedback: The provisions cover more than just eligibility and remittance transactions.

options.label: D
options.text: Only transactions between government programs like Medicare and Medicaid
options.feedback: The standards apply to all HIPAA-covered entities, not just government programs.

questions.stem: Protected Health Information (PHI) is only protected under HIPAA when it is stored electronically.
questions.feedback: PHI is protected under HIPAA regardless of its form — electronic, paper, or oral.

questions.stem: The minimum necessary standard applies to disclosures of PHI for treatment purposes between health care providers.
questions.feedback: Disclosures of PHI for treatment purposes between health care providers are exempt from the minimum necessary requirement.

questions.stem: A business associate must sign a written agreement to comply with HIPAA rules before handling PHI on behalf of a covered entity.
questions.feedback: A business associate is required to sign a written agreement (Business Associate Agreement) to comply with HIPAA rules before handling PHI on behalf of a covered entity.

questions.stem: The United States transitioned from ICD-9 to ICD-10 on October 1, 2015.
questions.feedback: The U.S. transitioned from ICD-9 to ICD-10 on October 1, 2015, expanding from approximately 14,000 diagnosis codes to approximately 69,000.

questions.stem: An in-network provider has a contract with the patient's health insurer to provide services at pre-negotiated rates.
questions.feedback: An in-network provider has a contract with the patient's health insurer to provide services at pre-negotiated rates, which results in lower out-of-pocket costs for the patient.

questions.stem: Which of the following best defines Protected Health Information (PHI)?
questions.feedback: PHI is individually identifiable health information that relates to a person's past, present, or future physical or mental health, the provision of health care, or payment for health care.

options.label: A
options.text: Any medical data stored in a hospital's computer system
options.feedback: Not all data in a hospital computer is PHI; it must be individually identifiable and related to health, care, or payment.

options.label: B
options.text: Individually identifiable health information relating to a person's health, care, or payment for care
options.feedback: PHI is individually identifiable health information that relates to a person's past, present, or future physical or mental health, the provision of health care, or payment for health care.

options.label: C
options.text: A patient's name and address regardless of whether it is linked to health data
options.feedback: A name and address alone, without a link to health information, is not PHI.

options.label: D
options.text: Only electronic records that contain diagnosis codes
options.feedback: PHI is not limited to electronic records or diagnosis codes; it covers all forms and types of health information.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a common identifier included in PHI?
questions.feedback: PHI includes common identifiers such as a patient's name, address, date of birth, and Social Security Number when they are associated with health information.

options.label: A
options.feedback: A favorite color is not a health identifier and is not part of PHI.

options.label: B
options.text: A patient's Social Security Number linked to health information
options.feedback: PHI includes common identifiers such as a patient's name, address, date of birth, and Social Security Number when they are associated with health information.

options.label: C
options.text: The brand of medical equipment used during treatment
options.feedback: An equipment brand name does not identify an individual patient.

options.label: D
options.text: The name of the hospital's CEO
options.feedback: The hospital CEO's name is not individually identifiable health information about a patient.

questions.stem: What does the HIPAA Privacy Rule establish?
questions.feedback: The HIPAA Privacy Rule establishes national standards for protecting individually identifiable health information held or transmitted by covered entities and their business associates.

options.label: A
options.text: Technical standards for encrypting electronic health records
options.feedback: Technical standards for electronic data are addressed by the HIPAA Security Rule, not the Privacy Rule.

options.label: B
options.text: National standards for protecting individually identifiable health information
options.feedback: The HIPAA Privacy Rule establishes national standards for protecting individually identifiable health information held or transmitted by covered entities and their business associates.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Reimbursement rates are set by CMS fee schedules, not by the Privacy Rule.

options.label: D
options.text: Licensing requirements for health care providers
options.feedback: Provider licensing is governed by state agencies, not the Privacy Rule.

questions.stem: What does the minimum necessary standard require covered entities to do?
questions.feedback: The minimum necessary standard requires covered entities to make reasonable efforts to limit the use and disclosure of PHI to the minimum amount needed to accomplish the intended purpose.

options.label: A
options.text: Share all available PHI to ensure thorough treatment
options.feedback: Sharing all available PHI contradicts the minimum necessary standard; only the needed information should be shared.

options.label: B
options.text: Limit the use and disclosure of PHI to the minimum amount needed for the intended purpose
options.feedback: The minimum necessary standard requires covered entities to make reasonable efforts to limit the use and disclosure of PHI to the minimum amount needed to accomplish the intended purpose.

options.label: C
options.text: Store the minimum number of patient records required by law
options.feedback: The standard relates to limiting disclosures, not to the number of records stored.

options.label: D
options.text: Require patients to provide the minimum amount of personal information
options.feedback: The standard applies to how covered entities handle PHI, not to what patients provide.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the three types of HIPAA covered entities?
questions.feedback: HIPAA defines three types of covered entities: health plans, health care clearinghouses, and health care providers who transmit health information electronically. Pharmaceutical manufacturers are not covered entities.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Health plans are one of the three types of HIPAA covered entities.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Health care clearinghouses are one of the three types of HIPAA covered entities.

options.label: C
options.feedback: HIPAA defines three types of covered entities: health plans, health care clearinghouses, and health care providers who transmit health information electronically. Pharmaceutical manufacturers are not covered entities.

options.label: D
options.text: Health care providers who transmit health information electronically
options.feedback: Health care providers who transmit health information electronically are one of the three types of HIPAA covered entities.

questions.stem: Which three categories of safeguards does the HIPAA Security Rule require?
questions.feedback: The HIPAA Security Rule requires three categories of safeguards: administrative (policies and procedures), physical (protection of facilities and equipment), and technical (technology controls for access and data protection).

options.label: A
options.feedback: The HIPAA Security Rule requires three categories of safeguards: administrative (policies and procedures), physical (protection of facilities and equipment), and technical (technology controls for access and data protection).

options.label: B
options.feedback: Clinical, financial, and operational are not the safeguard categories defined by the Security Rule.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Federal, state, and local refer to levels of government, not Security Rule safeguard categories.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Preventive, detective, and corrective are general security control types but not the categories specified in the HIPAA Security Rule.

questions.stem: Under the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, within how many days must a covered entity notify affected individuals of a breach of unsecured PHI?
questions.feedback: The Breach Notification Rule requires covered entities to notify affected individuals without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovering the breach.

options.label: A
options.feedback: 30 days is the timeframe for responding to a patient's access request, not for breach notification.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The Breach Notification Rule requires covered entities to notify affected individuals without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovering the breach.

options.label: C
options.feedback: 90 days exceeds the maximum allowed notification period for a breach.

options.label: D
options.feedback: 120 days exceeds the maximum allowed notification period for a breach.

questions.stem: Which coding system is used to classify medical diagnoses?
questions.feedback: ICD (International Classification of Diseases) codes are standardized codes used to represent medical diagnoses and inpatient procedures on claims and medical records.

options.label: A
options.feedback: CPT codes identify medical services and procedures performed by providers, not diagnoses.

options.label: B
options.feedback: ICD (International Classification of Diseases) codes are standardized codes used to represent medical diagnoses and inpatient procedures on claims and medical records.

options.label: C
options.feedback: HCPCS Level II codes cover products, supplies, and services such as ambulance services and DME.

options.label: D
options.feedback: NPI is a provider identification number, not a coding system for diagnoses.

questions.stem: What type of codes are CPT codes, and who maintains them?
questions.feedback: CPT (Current Procedural Terminology) codes are 5-digit numeric codes maintained by the American Medical Association (AMA) that identify medical services and procedures.

options.label: A
options.text: Diagnosis codes maintained by the World Health Organization
options.feedback: ICD codes, not CPT codes, classify diagnoses. The World Health Organization maintains ICD internationally.

options.label: B
options.feedback: CMS maintains HCPCS Level II codes for supplies and equipment, not CPT codes.

options.label: C
options.text: 5-digit procedure codes maintained by the American Medical Association (AMA)
options.feedback: CPT (Current Procedural Terminology) codes are 5-digit numeric codes maintained by the American Medical Association (AMA) that identify medical services and procedures.

options.label: D
options.feedback: ICD-10 codes can be up to 7 characters; CPT codes are 5-digit numeric codes maintained by the AMA.

questions.stem: What is an Electronic Health Record (EHR)?
questions.feedback: An EHR is a digital version of a patient's medical history maintained by the provider over time, including demographics, medications, vital signs, immunizations, lab results, and radiology reports.

options.label: A
options.text: A paper chart stored in a filing cabinet at the provider's office
options.feedback: A paper chart is a traditional medical record, not an electronic health record.

options.label: B
options.text: A digital version of a patient's medical history maintained by the provider over time
options.feedback: An EHR is a digital version of a patient's medical history maintained by the provider over time, including demographics, medications, vital signs, immunizations, lab results, and radiology reports.

options.label: C
options.text: An insurance claim submitted electronically to a health plan
options.feedback: An electronic claim is a billing transaction, not an EHR.

options.label: D
options.text: A billing statement sent to the patient after a visit
options.feedback: A billing statement is a financial document, not a comprehensive digital medical record.

questions.stem: PHI includes information about a person's past, present, or future physical or mental health condition.
questions.feedback: By definition, PHI covers individually identifiable health information relating to past, present, or future health, care, or payment for care.

questions.stem: A patient's date of birth becomes PHI only when it is linked to health information.
questions.feedback: Common identifiers such as date of birth become PHI when they are associated with health information about the individual.

questions.stem: A conversation between a doctor and a nurse about a patient's diagnosis is not considered PHI because it is spoken aloud.
questions.feedback: PHI is protected under HIPAA regardless of form, including oral communications.

questions.stem: Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule, patients have the right to obtain a copy of their own protected health information.
questions.feedback: The HIPAA Privacy Rule gives individuals the right to access, inspect, and obtain a copy of their own PHI.

questions.stem: A health care clearinghouse is one of the three types of HIPAA covered entities.
questions.feedback: HIPAA defines three types of covered entities — health plans, health care clearinghouses, and certain health care providers.

questions.stem: The HIPAA Security Rule applies to all forms of PHI, including paper records and oral communications.
questions.feedback: The HIPAA Security Rule specifically protects electronic PHI (ePHI). The Privacy Rule, not the Security Rule, covers PHI in all forms.

questions.stem: Physical safeguards under the HIPAA Security Rule include policies for protecting facilities and equipment that store ePHI.
questions.feedback: Physical safeguards address the protection of physical facilities, workstations, and devices that store or access ePHI.

questions.stem: When a breach of unsecured PHI affects 500 or more residents of a state, the covered entity must notify prominent media outlets.
questions.feedback: The Breach Notification Rule requires media notification when a breach affects 500 or more residents of a state or jurisdiction.

questions.stem: Breaches of unsecured PHI that affect fewer than 500 individuals do not need to be reported to HHS at all.
questions.feedback: Smaller breaches (fewer than 500 individuals) must still be reported to HHS, but they are submitted annually rather than within 60 days.

questions.stem: ICD-10-PCS codes are used by all health care providers in every clinical setting to code diagnoses.
questions.feedback: ICD-10-PCS codes are used specifically for inpatient hospital procedures. ICD-10-CM codes are used by all providers to code diagnoses.

questions.stem: ICD-10 codes can be up to 7 characters long, providing more clinical detail than ICD-9 codes.
questions.feedback: ICD-10 codes can be up to 7 characters, compared to shorter ICD-9 codes, allowing more detail about location, severity, and type of condition.

questions.stem: CPT codes are maintained by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS).
questions.feedback: CPT codes are maintained by the American Medical Association (AMA), not CMS. HCPCS Level II codes are maintained by CMS.

questions.stem: Patients typically pay less when they receive care from an out-of-network provider compared to an in-network provider.
questions.feedback: Out-of-network providers do not have pre-negotiated rates with the insurer, so patients typically pay significantly more.

questions.stem: One benefit of Electronic Health Records is reducing the duplication of tests for patients.
questions.feedback: EHRs help reduce duplication of tests by making health information available electronically to authorized providers.

questions.stem: HCPCS Level II codes are the same as CPT codes.
questions.feedback: HCPCS Level I consists of CPT codes, not Level II. Level II covers products, supplies, and services not included in CPT.

questions.stem: A business associate may handle PHI on behalf of a covered entity without any written agreement as long as both parties verbally agree.
questions.feedback: A business associate must sign a written Business Associate Agreement before handling PHI on behalf of a covered entity.

questions.stem: The HIPAA Security Rule requires covered entities and business associates to ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of ePHI.
questions.feedback: The Security Rule establishes national standards to protect ePHI, requiring confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

questions.stem: Certified EHR technology stores patient data in an unstructured format that requires manual sorting by providers.
questions.feedback: Certified EHR technology stores patient data in a structured format that allows easy retrieval, transfer, and use.

questions.stem: The transition from ICD-9 to ICD-10 expanded the number of available diagnosis codes from approximately 14,000 to approximately 69,000.
questions.feedback: The ICD-10 transition dramatically increased the number of available diagnosis codes from roughly 14,000 to roughly 69,000.

questions.stem: Which of the following categories of information does PHI relate to?
questions.feedback: PHI relates to a person's past, present, or future health, the provision of health care, or payment for health care.

options.label: A
options.text: A person's health condition, health care provision, or payment for health care
options.feedback: PHI relates to a person's past, present, or future health, the provision of health care, or payment for health care.

options.label: B
options.text: A person's employment history and salary information
options.feedback: Employment and salary data are not health information and are not covered by HIPAA.

options.label: C
options.text: A person's educational background and academic records
options.feedback: Educational records fall under FERPA, not HIPAA.

options.label: D
options.text: A person's criminal record and court proceedings
options.feedback: Criminal records are not health information protected under HIPAA.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT typically considered a common PHI identifier?
questions.feedback: A blood type by itself, with no link to an individual's identity, is not PHI. PHI requires both health data and an identifier.

options.label: A
options.feedback: A patient's name linked to health information is a common PHI identifier.

options.label: B
options.text: Patient's blood type when not linked to any identifying information
options.feedback: A blood type by itself, with no link to an individual's identity, is not PHI. PHI requires both health data and an identifier.

options.label: C
options.text: Patient's Social Security Number linked to health data
options.feedback: A Social Security Number linked to health data is a classic example of PHI.

options.label: D
options.text: Patient's home address linked to health data
options.feedback: A home address linked to health information qualifies as PHI.

questions.stem: PHI is protected under HIPAA in which of the following forms?
questions.feedback: HIPAA protects PHI regardless of its form — electronic, paper, or oral.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Electronic-only protection would leave paper records and spoken information unprotected.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Oral communications containing PHI are also protected under HIPAA.

options.label: C
options.feedback: HIPAA protects PHI regardless of its form — electronic, paper, or oral.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Electronic PHI is also protected under HIPAA, particularly by the Security Rule.

questions.stem: The HIPAA Privacy Rule applies to which of the following?
questions.feedback: The Privacy Rule establishes standards for covered entities and their business associates regarding the protection of PHI.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The Privacy Rule applies to all covered entities, not just hospitals and physician offices.

options.label: B
options.text: Covered entities and their business associates
options.feedback: The Privacy Rule establishes standards for covered entities and their business associates regarding the protection of PHI.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Health plans are only one type of covered entity. Providers and clearinghouses are also covered.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The Privacy Rule applies broadly to covered entities in both the private and public sectors.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the minimum necessary standard?
questions.feedback: The minimum necessary standard requires covered entities to limit PHI use and disclosure to the minimum needed for the intended purpose.

options.label: A
options.text: To require the smallest possible electronic file size for health records
options.feedback: The standard relates to PHI disclosure limits, not file size or data storage.

options.label: B
options.text: To limit PHI use and disclosure to the minimum amount needed for the intended purpose
options.feedback: The minimum necessary standard requires covered entities to limit PHI use and disclosure to the minimum needed for the intended purpose.

options.label: C
options.text: To ensure that only the minimum number of staff members have access to medical equipment
options.feedback: Staffing levels for equipment access are not related to the minimum necessary standard.

options.label: D
options.text: To set the minimum number of patient visits required before PHI can be created
options.feedback: The standard governs how much PHI is shared, not how many patient visits occur.

questions.stem: Which type of PHI disclosure is exempt from the minimum necessary requirement?
questions.feedback: Treatment-related disclosures between health care providers are specifically exempt from the minimum necessary standard.

options.label: A
options.text: Disclosures for health plan marketing campaigns
options.feedback: Marketing disclosures are not exempt from HIPAA requirements.

options.label: B
options.text: Disclosures for treatment purposes between health care providers
options.feedback: Treatment-related disclosures between health care providers are specifically exempt from the minimum necessary standard.

options.label: C
options.text: Disclosures to law enforcement for non-urgent inquiries
options.feedback: Law enforcement disclosures are not broadly exempt from the minimum necessary standard.

options.label: D
options.text: Disclosures to employers for workplace wellness programs
options.feedback: Employer wellness disclosures are not exempt from the minimum necessary requirement.

questions.stem: Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule, which right do patients have regarding their PHI?
questions.feedback: The Privacy Rule grants individuals the right to access, inspect, and obtain a copy of their own PHI.

options.label: A
options.text: The right to delete any information from the provider's records permanently
options.feedback: Patients can request amendments, but providers are not required to delete records.

options.label: B
options.text: The right to access, inspect, and obtain a copy of their PHI
options.feedback: The Privacy Rule grants individuals the right to access, inspect, and obtain a copy of their own PHI.

options.label: C
options.text: The right to prevent any provider from viewing their PHI
options.feedback: Patients cannot universally block all provider access to PHI needed for treatment.

options.label: D
options.text: The right to edit their medical records directly without provider involvement
options.feedback: Patients may request amendments, but they cannot directly edit their medical records.

questions.stem: Which of the following qualifies as a HIPAA covered entity?
questions.feedback: Health care providers who transmit health information electronically in connection with covered transactions are one of the three types of covered entities.

options.label: A
options.text: A health care provider who transmits health information electronically in connection with covered transactions
options.feedback: Health care providers who transmit health information electronically in connection with covered transactions are one of the three types of covered entities.

options.label: B
options.text: A medical device manufacturer that sells equipment to hospitals
options.feedback: Medical device manufacturers are not covered entities unless they also function as a provider, plan, or clearinghouse.

options.label: C
options.text: A pharmaceutical company that develops prescription drugs
options.feedback: Pharmaceutical companies are not covered entities under HIPAA.

options.label: D
options.text: A health and wellness blog that publishes medical advice
options.feedback: A blog publishing medical advice is not a covered entity.

questions.stem: What distinguishes a health care clearinghouse from other covered entities?
questions.feedback: Health care clearinghouses process health information received from one entity into a standard format for another entity.

options.label: A
options.text: It provides direct patient care in a clinical setting
options.feedback: Clearinghouses do not provide direct patient care — that is the role of providers.

options.label: B
options.text: It processes health information received from another entity into a standard format
options.feedback: Health care clearinghouses process health information received from one entity into a standard format for another entity.

options.label: C
options.text: It sells health insurance policies to individuals
options.feedback: Selling insurance policies is the function of health plans, not clearinghouses.

options.label: D
options.text: It manufactures medical equipment for hospitals
options.feedback: Equipment manufacturing is not a clearinghouse function.

questions.stem: What must a business associate sign before handling PHI on behalf of a covered entity?
questions.feedback: A business associate must sign a written Business Associate Agreement (BAA) to comply with HIPAA rules before handling PHI.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Patient consent forms are signed by patients, not business associates.

options.label: B
options.feedback: A business associate must sign a written Business Associate Agreement (BAA) to comply with HIPAA rules before handling PHI.

options.label: C
options.feedback: A medical license is for health care providers, not business associates.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Provider enrollment is for providers participating in health plans, not for business associates.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a business associate?
questions.feedback: A billing company that handles PHI on behalf of a covered entity is a business associate and must have a BAA in place.

options.label: A
options.text: A patient receiving treatment at a hospital
options.feedback: A patient is not a business associate — they are the individual whose PHI is being protected.

options.label: B
options.text: A billing company that processes claims containing PHI for a physician's office
options.feedback: A billing company that handles PHI on behalf of a covered entity is a business associate and must have a BAA in place.

options.label: C
options.text: A nurse providing direct care to a patient in a hospital
options.feedback: A nurse providing care is part of the covered entity (the provider), not a separate business associate.

options.label: D
options.text: A health insurer paying claims to a provider
options.feedback: A health insurer is itself a covered entity, not a business associate of another covered entity in this context.

questions.stem: The HIPAA Security Rule specifically protects which type of information?
questions.feedback: The Security Rule specifically addresses the protection of electronic protected health information (ePHI).

options.label: A
options.text: All patient information in any form
options.feedback: The Security Rule focuses on ePHI. The Privacy Rule provides broader protection for PHI in all forms.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The Security Rule specifically addresses the protection of electronic protected health information (ePHI).

options.label: C
options.feedback: Paper records are covered by the Privacy Rule, not the Security Rule.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Oral communications are covered by the Privacy Rule, not the Security Rule.

questions.stem: Which of the following is one of the three core objectives the HIPAA Security Rule aims to ensure for ePHI?
questions.feedback: The Security Rule requires ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of ePHI.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Profitability is a business objective, not a security objective under HIPAA.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The Security Rule requires ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of ePHI.

options.label: C
options.feedback: While HIPAA includes portability provisions, the Security Rule focuses on confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Marketability is not a security objective under HIPAA.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of an administrative safeguard under the HIPAA Security Rule?
questions.feedback: Administrative safeguards include policies and procedures such as workforce training on handling ePHI.

options.label: A
options.text: Installing locks on server room doors
options.feedback: Locks on server rooms are a physical safeguard.

options.label: B
options.text: Implementing workforce training policies on handling ePHI
options.feedback: Administrative safeguards include policies and procedures such as workforce training on handling ePHI.

options.label: C
options.text: Using encryption software to protect data in transit
options.feedback: Encryption is a technical safeguard.

options.label: D
options.text: Placing fire extinguishers near data storage equipment
options.feedback: Fire protection for equipment is a physical safeguard.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a technical safeguard under the HIPAA Security Rule?
questions.feedback: Technical safeguards are technology-based controls such as access controls, user authentication, and encryption.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Background checks are an administrative safeguard.

options.label: B
options.text: Storing backup tapes in a locked offsite location
options.feedback: Offsite storage of backup tapes is a physical safeguard.

options.label: C
options.text: Implementing access controls that require unique user IDs and passwords
options.feedback: Technical safeguards are technology-based controls such as access controls, user authentication, and encryption.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Staff training is an administrative safeguard.

questions.stem: Under the Breach Notification Rule, what is the maximum number of days a covered entity has to notify affected individuals after discovering a breach of unsecured PHI?
questions.feedback: The Breach Notification Rule requires notification without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovery.

options.label: A
options.feedback: 30 days is shorter than the required notification window.

options.label: B
