options.feedback: 45 days is not the correct timeframe under the rule.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The Breach Notification Rule requires notification without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovery.

options.label: D
options.feedback: 90 days exceeds the maximum allowed notification period.

questions.stem: What triggers the requirement for a covered entity to notify prominent media outlets about a breach?
questions.feedback: Media notification is required when a breach affects 500 or more residents of a single state or jurisdiction.

options.label: A
options.text: Any breach affecting at least one individual
options.feedback: Media notification is not required for every breach — only large-scale breaches trigger this requirement.

options.label: B
options.text: A breach affecting 500 or more residents of a state or jurisdiction
options.feedback: Media notification is required when a breach affects 500 or more residents of a single state or jurisdiction.

options.label: C
options.text: A breach involving electronic records only
options.feedback: The format of the records is not what triggers media notification — it is the number of affected individuals.

options.label: D
options.text: A breach that occurs at a federal government facility
options.feedback: The type of facility does not determine whether media notification is required.

questions.stem: How are breaches of unsecured PHI affecting fewer than 500 individuals reported to HHS?
questions.feedback: Smaller breaches affecting fewer than 500 individuals are reported to HHS on an annual basis.

options.label: A
options.text: They do not need to be reported to HHS
options.feedback: All breaches of unsecured PHI must be reported to HHS regardless of size.

options.label: B
options.text: They must be reported within 60 days of discovery
options.feedback: The 60-day individual notification requirement applies, but HHS reporting for small breaches is annual.

options.label: C
options.text: They are submitted to HHS annually
options.feedback: Smaller breaches affecting fewer than 500 individuals are reported to HHS on an annual basis.

options.label: D
options.text: They are reported to state authorities instead of HHS
options.feedback: HHS reporting is required for all breaches. State notification may also apply but does not replace HHS reporting.

questions.stem: What does the abbreviation ICD stand for?
questions.feedback: ICD stands for International Classification of Diseases, the standardized system for coding diagnoses.

options.label: A
options.feedback: There is no standardized coding system called the Integrated Clinical Database.

options.label: B
options.feedback: ICD stands for International Classification of Diseases, the standardized system for coding diagnoses.

options.label: C
options.feedback: ICD does not stand for Internal Code for Diagnoses.

options.label: D
options.feedback: ICD is a diagnostic classification system, not an insurance claims documentation system.

questions.stem: ICD codes are primarily used to represent which of the following on claims and medical records?
questions.feedback: ICD codes are standardized codes used to represent medical diagnoses and inpatient procedures on claims and records.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Provider credentials are not represented by ICD codes.

options.label: B
options.feedback: ICD codes are standardized codes used to represent medical diagnoses and inpatient procedures on claims and records.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Insurance premiums are financial amounts, not coded with ICD.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Drug dosages are not classified using ICD codes.

questions.stem: On what date did the United States transition from ICD-9 to ICD-10?
questions.feedback: The U.S. transitioned from ICD-9 to ICD-10 on October 1, 2015.

options.label: A
options.feedback: January 1, 2010 was not the ICD-10 transition date.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The U.S. transitioned from ICD-9 to ICD-10 on October 1, 2015.

options.label: C
options.feedback: July 1, 2013 was not the transition date, though it was an earlier proposed deadline.

options.label: D
options.feedback: March 1, 2018 is well after the actual transition date.

questions.stem: Approximately how many diagnosis codes does ICD-10 contain, compared to ICD-9?
questions.feedback: ICD-9 had approximately 14,000 diagnosis codes; ICD-10 expanded this to approximately 69,000.

options.label: A
options.feedback: This reverses the numbers. ICD-10 has more codes than ICD-9.

options.label: B
options.feedback: ICD-9 had approximately 14,000 diagnosis codes; ICD-10 expanded this to approximately 69,000.

options.label: C
options.feedback: These numbers do not reflect the actual code counts for either system.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Neither 30,000 nor 100,000 reflects the actual code counts.

questions.stem: Which coding system is used specifically for inpatient hospital procedures?
questions.feedback: ICD-10-PCS (Procedure Coding System) is used specifically for coding inpatient hospital procedures.

options.label: A
options.feedback: ICD-10-CM is used for diagnosis coding across all settings, not specifically for inpatient procedures.

options.label: B
options.feedback: ICD-10-PCS (Procedure Coding System) is used specifically for coding inpatient hospital procedures.

options.label: C
options.feedback: CPT codes identify medical services and procedures but are not specific to inpatient hospital procedures.

options.label: D
options.feedback: HCPCS Level II codes cover supplies, equipment, and services not included in CPT.

questions.stem: What does the abbreviation CM stand for in ICD-10-CM?
questions.feedback: CM stands for Clinical Modification. ICD-10-CM codes are used by all providers to code diagnoses.

options.label: A
options.feedback: CM does not stand for Comprehensive Medicine.

options.label: B
options.feedback: CM stands for Clinical Modification. ICD-10-CM codes are used by all providers to code diagnoses.

options.label: C
options.feedback: CM does not stand for Claims Management.

options.label: D
options.feedback: CM does not stand for Certified Medical.

questions.stem: What is the maximum character length of an ICD-10 code?
questions.feedback: ICD-10 codes can be up to 7 characters long, providing detail about location, severity, and type of condition.

options.label: A
options.feedback: 3 characters would be the minimum category code, not the maximum length.

options.label: B
options.feedback: 5 characters is the length of CPT codes, not the maximum for ICD-10.

options.label: C
options.feedback: ICD-10 codes can be up to 7 characters long, providing detail about location, severity, and type of condition.

options.label: D
options.feedback: ICD-10 codes do not extend to 10 characters.

questions.stem: What advantage does the longer character length of ICD-10 codes provide over ICD-9?
questions.feedback: The expanded character length of ICD-10 allows more clinical detail about location, severity, and type of condition.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Code length does not directly determine claims processing speed.

options.label: B
options.text: More detail about the location, severity, and type of a condition
options.feedback: The expanded character length of ICD-10 allows more clinical detail about location, severity, and type of condition.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Insurance premiums are not determined by the length of diagnosis codes.

options.label: D
options.text: Fewer codes for providers to memorize
options.feedback: ICD-10 actually has far more codes than ICD-9, not fewer.

questions.stem: HCPCS is a standardized coding system with how many levels?
questions.feedback: HCPCS has two levels — Level I (CPT codes) and Level II (products, supplies, and services not included in CPT).

options.label: A
options.feedback: HCPCS has two levels, not one.

options.label: B
options.feedback: HCPCS has two levels — Level I (CPT codes) and Level II (products, supplies, and services not included in CPT).

options.label: C
options.feedback: HCPCS has two levels. There was historically a Level III, but it was discontinued.

options.label: D
options.feedback: HCPCS does not have four levels.

questions.stem: What does HCPCS Level II cover that is not included in CPT?
questions.feedback: HCPCS Level II covers products, supplies, and services not included in CPT, such as ambulance services and durable medical equipment.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Surgical procedures are typically coded using CPT or ICD-10-PCS, not HCPCS Level II.

options.label: B
options.text: Products, supplies, and services such as ambulance rides and durable medical equipment
options.feedback: HCPCS Level II covers products, supplies, and services not included in CPT, such as ambulance services and durable medical equipment.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Mental health diagnoses are coded with ICD codes, not HCPCS Level II.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Lab test interpretations are typically coded with CPT, not HCPCS Level II.

questions.stem: How many digits does a CPT code contain?
questions.feedback: CPT codes are 5-digit numeric codes that identify medical services and procedures.

options.label: A
options.feedback: CPT codes are longer than 3 digits.

options.label: B
options.feedback: 4 digits is the numeric portion of a HCPCS Level II code, but CPT codes have 5 digits.

options.label: C
options.feedback: CPT codes are 5-digit numeric codes that identify medical services and procedures.

options.label: D
options.feedback: 7 characters is the maximum length for ICD-10 codes, not CPT.

questions.stem: What organization maintains and publishes CPT codes?
questions.feedback: The American Medical Association (AMA) develops and maintains CPT codes.

options.label: A
options.text: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS)
options.feedback: CMS maintains HCPCS Level II codes, not CPT codes.

options.label: B
options.feedback: WHO maintains the ICD classification system, not CPT.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The American Medical Association (AMA) develops and maintains CPT codes.

options.label: D
options.text: Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
options.feedback: HHS oversees HIPAA enforcement but does not maintain CPT codes.

questions.stem: What is the format of a HCPCS Level II code?
questions.feedback: HCPCS Level II codes consist of one alphabetical letter followed by 4 numeric digits.

options.label: A
options.feedback: 5 numeric digits describes a CPT code, not a HCPCS Level II code.

options.label: B
options.text: One alphabetical letter followed by 4 numeric digits
options.feedback: HCPCS Level II codes consist of one alphabetical letter followed by 4 numeric digits.

options.label: C
options.feedback: 7 alphanumeric characters describes the maximum length of an ICD-10 code.

options.label: D
options.text: Two letters followed by 3 numeric digits
options.feedback: HCPCS Level II codes begin with one letter, not two.

questions.stem: Who maintains HCPCS Level II codes?
questions.feedback: CMS maintains HCPCS Level II codes to identify items such as ambulance services, durable medical equipment, and prosthetics.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The AMA maintains CPT codes (HCPCS Level I), not Level II.

options.label: B
options.feedback: WHO maintains the ICD system, not HCPCS Level II.

options.label: C
options.text: The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS)
options.feedback: CMS maintains HCPCS Level II codes to identify items such as ambulance services, durable medical equipment, and prosthetics.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The AHA is not responsible for maintaining HCPCS Level II codes.

questions.stem: Which of the following items would most likely be coded using a HCPCS Level II code?
questions.feedback: HCPCS Level II codes identify items like durable medical equipment, ambulance services, and prosthetics.

options.label: A
options.text: An office visit with a primary care physician
options.feedback: An office visit would typically be coded with a CPT code.

options.label: B
options.text: A wheelchair provided to a patient as durable medical equipment
options.feedback: HCPCS Level II codes identify items like durable medical equipment, ambulance services, and prosthetics.

options.label: C
options.feedback: A diagnosis is coded using an ICD code, not HCPCS Level II.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Outpatient surgery is typically coded with CPT codes.

questions.stem: What is the primary financial benefit to patients of using an in-network provider?
questions.feedback: In-network providers have pre-negotiated rates with the insurer, which results in lower out-of-pocket costs for patients.

options.label: A
options.text: The provider waives all fees for insured patients
options.feedback: In-network providers do not waive all fees — patients still have cost-sharing responsibilities.

options.label: B
options.text: The provider charges pre-negotiated rates, resulting in lower out-of-pocket costs
options.feedback: In-network providers have pre-negotiated rates with the insurer, which results in lower out-of-pocket costs for patients.

options.label: C
options.text: The patient receives a tax credit for each in-network visit
options.feedback: Tax credits are not tied to using in-network providers.

options.label: D
options.text: The patient's deductible is automatically waived
options.feedback: Deductibles are not automatically waived for in-network visits.

questions.stem: Why do patients typically pay more when using an out-of-network provider?
questions.feedback: Without a contract, the provider is not bound to pre-negotiated rates, so the patient bears higher costs.

options.label: A
options.text: Out-of-network providers charge higher taxes on their services
options.feedback: Provider charges are not taxes and are not related to tax rates.

options.label: B
options.text: The provider does not have a contract with the insurer, so there are no pre-negotiated rates
options.feedback: Without a contract, the provider is not bound to pre-negotiated rates, so the patient bears higher costs.

options.label: C
options.text: Out-of-network providers use more expensive medical equipment
options.feedback: Equipment cost is not the reason patients pay more for out-of-network care.

options.label: D
options.text: The patient's insurance policy is cancelled when they go out of network
options.feedback: Using an out-of-network provider does not cancel the patient's insurance policy.

questions.stem: What does it mean for a provider to be "in-network"?
questions.feedback: An in-network provider has a contract with the patient's health insurer to provide services at pre-negotiated rates.

options.label: A
options.text: The provider is located within the same state as the patient
options.feedback: Network status is determined by a contractual relationship with an insurer, not geographic location.

options.label: B
options.text: The provider has a contract with the patient's health insurer to provide services at agreed-upon rates
options.feedback: An in-network provider has a contract with the patient's health insurer to provide services at pre-negotiated rates.

options.label: C
options.text: The provider accepts only Medicare patients
options.feedback: In-network status applies to any insurer, not just Medicare.

options.label: D
options.text: The provider works exclusively in a hospital setting
options.feedback: In-network status is about the contractual relationship, not the practice setting.

questions.stem: Which of the following is typically included in an Electronic Health Record (EHR)?
questions.feedback: EHRs include clinical data such as demographics, medications, vital signs, immunizations, lab results, and radiology reports.

options.label: A
options.text: A patient's credit score and financial history
options.feedback: Credit scores and financial history are not part of an EHR.

options.label: B
options.text: Demographics, medications, vital signs, and lab results
options.feedback: EHRs include clinical data such as demographics, medications, vital signs, immunizations, lab results, and radiology reports.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Provider insurance details are administrative records, not EHR content.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Hospital revenue reports are financial documents, not part of an EHR.

questions.stem: How do EHRs help improve the quality of patient care?
questions.feedback: EHRs reduce test duplication, decrease delays, and improve accuracy by making information available electronically to authorized providers.

options.label: A
options.text: By eliminating the need for physicians entirely
options.feedback: EHRs support physicians — they do not replace them.

options.label: B
options.text: By reducing duplication of tests, decreasing treatment delays, and improving record accuracy
options.feedback: EHRs reduce test duplication, decrease delays, and improve accuracy by making information available electronically to authorized providers.

options.label: C
options.text: By allowing patients to diagnose their own conditions
options.feedback: EHRs provide information to providers; they do not enable patient self-diagnosis.

options.label: D
options.text: By replacing all in-person visits with telehealth sessions
options.feedback: EHRs are digital records, not telehealth platforms.

questions.stem: What is a key characteristic of certified EHR technology?
questions.feedback: Certified EHR technology stores data in a structured format that supports retrieval, transfer, and care decisions.

options.label: A
options.text: It stores data in handwritten format for authenticity
options.feedback: Certified EHRs store data digitally in a structured format, not handwritten.

options.label: B
options.text: It stores patient data in a structured format for easy retrieval, transfer, and use
options.feedback: Certified EHR technology stores data in a structured format that supports retrieval, transfer, and care decisions.

options.label: C
options.text: It restricts data access to a single provider at a time
options.feedback: EHRs are designed to support access by multiple authorized providers.

options.label: D
options.text: It requires data to be printed on paper before it can be shared
options.feedback: Certified EHRs enable electronic sharing without printing.

questions.stem: What type of standards does the HIPAA Privacy Rule establish?
questions.feedback: The Privacy Rule establishes national standards for the protection of individually identifiable health information.

options.label: A
options.text: Local hospital guidelines for visitor policies
options.feedback: The Privacy Rule sets national standards, not local hospital guidelines.

options.label: B
options.text: National standards for protecting individually identifiable health information
options.feedback: The Privacy Rule establishes national standards for the protection of individually identifiable health information.

options.label: C
options.feedback: HIPAA is a U.S. law, not an international pharmaceutical standard.

options.label: D
options.text: State-level regulations for medical school accreditation
options.feedback: Medical school accreditation is handled by separate organizations, not the HIPAA Privacy Rule.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a physical safeguard under the HIPAA Security Rule?
questions.feedback: Physical safeguards protect the physical facilities, workstations, and devices where ePHI is stored or accessed.

options.label: A
options.text: Assigning unique login credentials to each employee
options.feedback: Unique login credentials are a technical safeguard.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Risk assessments are an administrative safeguard.

options.label: C
options.text: Controlling physical access to facilities where ePHI is stored
options.feedback: Physical safeguards protect the physical facilities, workstations, and devices where ePHI is stored or accessed.

options.label: D
options.text: Encrypting emails that contain patient data
options.feedback: Email encryption is a technical safeguard.

questions.stem: The HIPAA Breach Notification Rule requires covered entities to notify affected individuals within what timeframe?
questions.feedback: The rule requires notification without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovering the breach.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The rule specifies "without unreasonable delay" but allows up to 60 days, not immediate notification.

options.label: B
options.text: Within 24 hours of discovering a breach
options.feedback: 24 hours is not the standard under the Breach Notification Rule.

options.label: C
options.text: Without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovery
options.feedback: The rule requires notification without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovering the breach.

options.label: D
options.text: Within one calendar year of the breach
options.feedback: One calendar year far exceeds the maximum allowed notification period.

questions.stem: How does an EHR differ from a traditional paper chart?
questions.feedback: An EHR is a digital version of a patient's medical history maintained by the provider over time, unlike a paper chart.

options.label: A
options.text: An EHR is a digital version of a patient's medical history maintained over time
options.feedback: An EHR is a digital version of a patient's medical history maintained by the provider over time, unlike a paper chart.

options.label: B
options.text: An EHR contains less information than a paper chart
options.feedback: EHRs typically contain more comprehensive and accessible data than paper charts.

options.label: C
options.text: An EHR can only be accessed by the patient, not the provider
options.feedback: EHRs are maintained by and accessible to authorized providers.

options.label: D
options.text: An EHR must be printed and filed in a cabinet to be valid
options.feedback: EHRs are valid in their electronic form and do not require printing.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the purpose of ICD codes in the healthcare system?
questions.feedback: ICD codes provide a standardized system for classifying medical diagnoses and inpatient procedures on claims and records.

options.label: A
options.text: To calculate provider salaries based on patient volume
options.feedback: Provider salaries are not determined by ICD codes.

options.label: B
options.text: To provide a standardized way to classify diagnoses and inpatient procedures
options.feedback: ICD codes provide a standardized system for classifying medical diagnoses and inpatient procedures on claims and records.

options.label: C
options.text: To determine which patients qualify for health insurance
options.feedback: Insurance eligibility is determined by plan rules, not ICD codes.

options.label: D
options.text: To rank hospitals by quality of care
options.feedback: Hospital rankings are not based on ICD codes.

questions.stem: Which scenario correctly applies the minimum necessary standard?
questions.feedback: Accessing only the specific data elements needed for the task (billing) is a proper application of the minimum necessary standard.

options.label: A
options.text: A billing clerk accesses only the diagnosis codes and procedure codes needed to process a claim
options.feedback: Accessing only the specific data elements needed for the task (billing) is a proper application of the minimum necessary standard.

options.label: B
options.text: A receptionist reads a patient's full psychiatric notes to schedule an appointment
options.feedback: A receptionist does not need psychiatric notes to schedule an appointment — this exceeds the minimum necessary.

options.label: C
options.text: An IT technician reviews all patient records to test a new software feature
options.feedback: Accessing all patient records for software testing is not limited to the minimum necessary.

options.label: D
options.text: A hospital administrator shares complete patient files with a marketing agency
options.feedback: Sharing complete patient files with a marketing agency violates the minimum necessary standard.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a way that EHRs help reduce delays in patient treatment?
questions.feedback: EHRs reduce treatment delays by making health information available electronically to authorized providers when and where it is needed.

options.label: A
options.text: By requiring patients to carry paper copies of their records to every appointment
options.feedback: EHRs eliminate the need for patients to carry paper records, not reinforce it.

options.label: B
options.text: By making health information available electronically to authorized providers
options.feedback: EHRs reduce treatment delays by making health information available electronically to authorized providers when and where it is needed.

options.label: C
options.text: By limiting the number of providers who can treat a patient
options.feedback: EHRs improve information access; they do not restrict which providers can treat a patient.

options.label: D
options.text: By replacing all diagnostic testing with automated algorithms
options.feedback: EHRs support care decisions but do not replace diagnostic testing.

questions.stem: A dependent is a family member covered under the subscriber's health insurance plan.
questions.feedback: A dependent is indeed a family member — such as a spouse, child, or domestic partner — covered under the subscriber's plan rather than holding their own policy.

questions.stem: Under the Affordable Care Act, children can remain on a parent's health plan until they reach age 30.
questions.feedback: Under the ACA, children can remain on a parent's plan until age 26, not age 30, regardless of marital status.

questions.stem: A covered entity must respond to a patient's request to access their health records within 30 days.
questions.feedback: Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule, a covered entity must respond to a patient's access request within 30 days of receiving it.

questions.stem: Informed consent can never be waived, even in a life-threatening emergency.
questions.feedback: Informed consent may be waived in life-threatening emergencies when there is not enough time to obtain consent and no authorized decision-maker is available.

questions.stem: An Explanation of Benefits (EOB) is a bill that the patient must pay immediately.
questions.feedback: An EOB is not a bill. It is a summary from the health plan showing what was billed, what the plan paid, and what the patient owes.

questions.stem: Who is the subscriber on a health insurance plan?
questions.feedback: The subscriber (also called the policyholder) is the primary individual who enrolls in and holds the health insurance contract with the insurance carrier.

options.label: A
options.text: A family member covered under someone else's plan
options.feedback: A family member covered under someone else's plan is a dependent, not the subscriber.

options.label: B
options.text: The primary individual who enrolls in and holds the insurance contract
options.feedback: The subscriber (also called the policyholder) is the primary individual who enrolls in and holds the health insurance contract with the insurance carrier.

options.label: D

questions.stem: The subscriber is also known as the policyholder on a health insurance plan.
questions.feedback: The subscriber and the policyholder are the same person — the primary individual who enrolls in and holds the health insurance contract.

questions.stem: A dependent must be employed by the same company as the subscriber to receive coverage.
questions.feedback: A dependent is a family member — such as a spouse, child, or domestic partner — covered under the subscriber's plan. Employment at the same company is not required.

questions.stem: Under the ACA, a child's marital status does not affect their ability to stay on a parent's health plan until age 26.

questions.stem: Outside of Open Enrollment, a person can enroll in a Marketplace plan at any time for any reason.

questions.stem: Most Special Enrollment Periods give a person 60 days to select a new health plan after a qualifying life event.

questions.stem: A provider may charge a patient a fee for searching for and retrieving their health records.

questions.stem: A patient can direct a covered entity to send their health information to a third party, such as another doctor.

questions.stem: The Notice of Privacy Practices is a bill sent to the patient after each doctor visit.
questions.feedback: The Notice of Privacy Practices is a plain-language document explaining how a covered entity may use and disclose the patient's health information. It is not a bill.

questions.stem: For children who cannot consent on their own, a parent or legal guardian provides informed consent for medical treatment.
questions.feedback: Competent adults consent for themselves, while a parent or legal guardian provides informed consent on behalf of a child.

questions.stem: A deductible is a fixed dollar amount paid at the time of each doctor visit.
questions.feedback: A fixed dollar amount paid at the time of a visit is a copayment. A deductible is the total amount a patient must pay out of pocket each year before the plan begins to pay its share.

questions.stem: Premiums must be paid regardless of whether the patient uses any medical services during that period.
questions.feedback: A premium is paid — usually monthly — to maintain health insurance coverage, whether or not the patient sees a doctor or uses any services.

questions.stem: After reaching the out-of-pocket maximum, the patient continues to pay coinsurance for covered services.
questions.feedback: Once the out-of-pocket maximum is reached, the health plan pays 100% of covered costs for the remainder of the plan year. The patient no longer pays coinsurance, copays, or deductible amounts.

questions.stem: Patients should compare their Explanation of Benefits with the provider's bill before making a payment.
questions.feedback: Patients should compare the EOB from their health plan with the bill from the provider to confirm that services and amounts match before paying.

questions.stem: Higher patient activation is associated with better health outcomes and lower costs.
questions.feedback: Research shows that patients who are more knowledgeable, skilled, and confident in managing their care tend to have better outcomes and incur lower healthcare costs.

questions.stem: Eligibility for employer-sponsored health insurance and Marketplace coverage is determined by the same set of rules.
questions.feedback: Employer-sponsored coverage eligibility is determined by the employer's plan rules, while Marketplace eligibility depends on factors such as income, household size, and residency.

questions.stem: Under HIPAA, patients have a legal, enforceable right to access their protected health information held by covered entities.
questions.feedback: The HIPAA Privacy Rule gives patients a legal and enforceable right to access and obtain copies of their PHI from covered entities such as doctors, hospitals, and health plans.

questions.stem: Any adult, regardless of mental competency, can give valid informed consent for medical treatment.

questions.stem: A copayment amount varies based on the total cost of the medical service.
questions.feedback: A copayment is a fixed dollar amount — for example $25 — that does not change based on the total cost of the service. The amount that varies with service cost is coinsurance.

questions.stem: What is Open Enrollment?
questions.feedback: Open Enrollment is the annual period when anyone can enroll in, renew, or change a Health Insurance Marketplace plan.

options.label: A
options.text: A penalty-free period after missing a premium payment
options.feedback: A grace period for missed premiums is different from Open Enrollment.

options.label: B
options.text: The annual period when anyone can enroll in, renew, or change a Marketplace plan
options.feedback: Open Enrollment is the annual period when anyone can enroll in, renew, or change a Health Insurance Marketplace plan.

options.label: C
options.text: A one-time enrollment opportunity available only to new employees
options.feedback: New employee enrollment is part of employer-sponsored plans, not the Marketplace Open Enrollment period.

options.label: D
options.text: A government program that automatically enrolls uninsured citizens
options.feedback: The Marketplace does not automatically enroll individuals.

questions.stem: A person loses their employer-sponsored health insurance. What enrollment opportunity becomes available?
questions.feedback: Losing existing health coverage is a qualifying life event that triggers a Special Enrollment Period, allowing the person to enroll in a Marketplace plan outside of Open Enrollment.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Losing coverage does not restart Open Enrollment; it triggers a Special Enrollment Period.

options.label: B
options.text: A Special Enrollment Period triggered by the qualifying life event
options.feedback: Losing existing health coverage is a qualifying life event that triggers a Special Enrollment Period, allowing the person to enroll in a Marketplace plan outside of Open Enrollment.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Medicaid enrollment depends on income eligibility, not automatic assignment.

options.label: D
options.text: A one-year waiting period before any new enrollment
options.feedback: There is no mandatory one-year waiting period; a 60-day SEP window applies.

questions.stem: Which factor determines eligibility for employer-sponsored health insurance?
questions.feedback: Eligibility for employer-sponsored health insurance is determined by the employer's plan rules, such as work hours and employment status.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Household income is relevant to Marketplace eligibility, not employer-sponsored plans.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Eligibility for employer-sponsored health insurance is determined by the employer's plan rules, such as work hours and employment status.

options.label: C
options.feedback: State residency affects Marketplace eligibility, not typically employer plan eligibility.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Credit history does not determine health insurance eligibility.

questions.stem: Which of the following can a patient request under HIPAA besides accessing their own records?
questions.feedback: Under HIPAA, patients have the right to request amendments to their health records if they believe the information is inaccurate or incomplete.

options.label: A
options.text: Deleting all records from every provider they have ever visited
options.feedback: HIPAA does not grant a blanket right to delete all records across all providers.

options.label: B
options.text: Amendments to inaccurate or incomplete information in their records
options.feedback: Under HIPAA, patients have the right to request amendments to their health records if they believe the information is inaccurate or incomplete.

options.label: C
options.text: A copy of another patient's records for comparison
options.feedback: Accessing another patient's records would violate HIPAA privacy rules.

options.label: D
options.text: A refund of all copayments made in the past year
options.feedback: Copayment refunds are a billing matter, not a HIPAA privacy right.

questions.stem: A patient asks their doctor's office to send medical records to a family member. Under HIPAA, which statement is correct?
questions.feedback: Under HIPAA, patients can direct a covered entity to send a copy of their health information to a third party of their choice, including a family member.

options.label: A
options.text: The office must refuse because records can only go to the patient
options.feedback: HIPAA allows patients to designate third-party recipients for their records.

options.label: B
options.text: The office can send the records only if the family member is a doctor
options.feedback: The third party does not need to be a medical professional.

options.label: C
options.text: The patient can direct the covered entity to send records to a third party of their choice
options.feedback: Under HIPAA, patients can direct a covered entity to send a copy of their health information to a third party of their choice, including a family member.

options.label: D
options.text: The office must get permission from the insurance company first
options.feedback: Insurance company permission is not required for patient-directed record sharing.

questions.stem: Which entity enforces HIPAA and receives patient privacy complaints?
questions.feedback: Patients have the right to file a HIPAA privacy complaint with HHS, which enforces the HIPAA Privacy Rule through its Office for Civil Rights.

options.label: A
options.text: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
options.feedback: The CDC focuses on disease prevention and public health, not HIPAA enforcement.

options.label: B
options.feedback: The FDA regulates food and drugs, not health information privacy.

options.label: C
options.text: The U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
options.feedback: Patients have the right to file a HIPAA privacy complaint with HHS, which enforces the HIPAA Privacy Rule through its Office for Civil Rights.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The SSA administers Social Security benefits, not HIPAA.

questions.stem: When must a patient receive a Notice of Privacy Practices?
questions.feedback: HIPAA requires covered entities to provide the Notice of Privacy Practices at or before the first delivery of service, ensuring patients know how their information may be used.

options.label: A
options.text: Only when they are admitted to a hospital
options.feedback: The notice is not limited to hospital admissions; all covered providers must issue it.

options.label: B
options.text: Only when they file an insurance claim
options.feedback: Filing a claim does not trigger the notice requirement; the first encounter does.

options.label: C
options.text: At or before their first encounter with a covered health care provider or health plan
options.feedback: HIPAA requires covered entities to provide the Notice of Privacy Practices at or before the first delivery of service, ensuring patients know how their information may be used.

options.label: D
options.text: Only when they request it in writing
options.feedback: The covered entity must proactively provide it, not wait for a written request.

questions.stem: What type of document is the Notice of Privacy Practices written in?
questions.feedback: The Notice of Privacy Practices is a plain-language document designed so patients can understand how their health information may be used and disclosed.

options.label: A
options.text: Medical terminology only clinicians can understand
options.feedback: The notice is specifically written to be understandable by patients, not just clinicians.

options.label: B
options.feedback: While it has legal significance, it is written in plain language for patient comprehension.

options.label: C
options.text: Plain language so patients can understand it
options.feedback: The Notice of Privacy Practices is a plain-language document designed so patients can understand how their health information may be used and disclosed.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The notice is a readable document, not an encrypted file.

questions.stem: Informed consent is best described as which of the following?
questions.feedback: Informed consent is a communication process in which a clinician explains the risks, benefits, and alternatives of a proposed treatment so the patient can make a voluntary, informed decision.

options.label: A
options.text: A one-time signature on an admission form
options.feedback: Informed consent is an ongoing dialogue, not just a single signature.

options.label: B
options.text: A communication process between clinician and patient about a proposed treatment
options.feedback: Informed consent is a communication process in which a clinician explains the risks, benefits, and alternatives of a proposed treatment so the patient can make a voluntary, informed decision.

options.label: C
options.text: A billing authorization from the insurance company
options.feedback: Insurance billing authorization is separate from clinical informed consent.

options.label: D
options.text: A legal contract between the hospital and the patient's employer
options.feedback: It is a clinician-patient process, not an employer contract.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT part of the informed consent discussion?
questions.feedback: Informed consent covers risks, benefits, and alternatives of a proposed treatment. The provider's salary is irrelevant to the consent process.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Risks are a core element of informed consent.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Benefits are a core element of informed consent.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Informed consent covers risks, benefits, and alternatives of a proposed treatment. The provider's salary is irrelevant to the consent process.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Alternatives are a core element of informed consent.

questions.stem: An unconscious patient arrives at the emergency room with a life-threatening injury and no family is present. What happens with informed consent?
questions.feedback: In life-threatening emergencies where the patient cannot consent and no authorized decision-maker is available, informed consent may be waived.

options.label: A
options.text: Treatment is delayed until a family member arrives
options.feedback: Delaying life-saving treatment to wait for family could result in harm or death.

options.label: B
options.text: The hospital must get a court order before treating
options.feedback: A court order is not required in emergency situations.

options.label: C
options.text: Informed consent may be waived so emergency treatment can proceed
options.feedback: In life-threatening emergencies where the patient cannot consent and no authorized decision-maker is available, informed consent may be waived.

options.label: D
options.text: The insurance company provides consent on the patient's behalf
options.feedback: Insurance companies do not provide medical consent.

questions.stem: Which cost-sharing component does a patient pay before the insurance plan begins covering services?
questions.feedback: The deductible is the amount a patient must pay out of pocket for covered services each year before the insurance plan begins paying its share.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Copayments are fixed per-visit fees, not the initial annual threshold.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Premiums maintain coverage but do not count toward the deductible.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The deductible is the amount a patient must pay out of pocket for covered services each year before the insurance plan begins paying its share.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Coinsurance applies after the deductible has been met.

questions.stem: How does a copayment differ from coinsurance?
questions.feedback: A copayment is a fixed dollar amount (e.g., $25), while coinsurance is a percentage of the service cost (e.g., 20%) paid after the deductible.

options.label: A
options.text: A copayment is a percentage and coinsurance is a fixed amount
options.feedback: This reverses the definitions. Copayment is the fixed amount and coinsurance is the percentage.

options.label: B
options.text: A copayment is a fixed dollar amount and coinsurance is a percentage of the cost
options.feedback: A copayment is a fixed dollar amount (e.g., $25), while coinsurance is a percentage of the service cost (e.g., 20%) paid after the deductible.

options.label: C
options.text: A copayment applies before the deductible and coinsurance applies after
options.feedback: While coinsurance typically applies after the deductible, the core difference is fixed amount versus percentage.

options.label: D
options.text: There is no difference; they are the same thing
options.feedback: They are distinct cost-sharing mechanisms.

questions.stem: Which cost-sharing component is paid monthly to keep health insurance coverage active?
questions.feedback: A premium is the amount paid — usually monthly — to maintain health insurance coverage, regardless of whether the patient uses any medical services.

options.label: A
options.feedback: A deductible is an annual out-of-pocket threshold, not a monthly payment.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Coinsurance is a percentage paid at the time of service, not a monthly fee.

options.label: C
options.feedback: A copayment is a per-visit charge, not a recurring monthly cost.

options.label: D
options.feedback: A premium is the amount paid — usually monthly — to maintain health insurance coverage, regardless of whether the patient uses any medical services.

questions.stem: Once a patient reaches their out-of-pocket maximum for the year, who pays for additional covered services?
questions.feedback: Once the out-of-pocket maximum is reached, the health plan pays 100% of covered costs for the remainder of the plan year.

options.label: A
options.text: The patient continues to pay copays only
options.feedback: After reaching the out-of-pocket maximum, the patient pays nothing more for covered services.

options.label: B
options.text: The health plan pays 100% of covered costs
options.feedback: Once the out-of-pocket maximum is reached, the health plan pays 100% of covered costs for the remainder of the plan year.

options.label: C
options.text: Costs are split 50/50 between the patient and the plan
options.feedback: There is no 50/50 split; the plan covers 100%.

options.label: D
options.text: The patient must purchase supplemental insurance
options.feedback: Supplemental insurance is not required once the out-of-pocket maximum is met.

questions.stem: A patient receives an EOB that lists a $500 charge, $350 paid by the plan, and $150 as the patient's responsibility. What should the patient do next?
questions.feedback: Patients should compare the EOB from their health plan with the provider's bill to confirm that services and amounts match before making any payment.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The patient owes $150, not $500, and should verify with the provider bill first.

options.label: B
options.text: Ignore the EOB since it is not a bill
options.feedback: While the EOB is not a bill, the patient should use it to verify the provider's bill.

options.label: C
options.text: Compare the EOB with the provider's bill to confirm the amounts match before paying
options.feedback: Patients should compare the EOB from their health plan with the provider's bill to confirm that services and amounts match before making any payment.

options.label: D
options.text: Contact the IRS to report the charge
options.feedback: The IRS does not handle medical billing disputes.

questions.stem: What is patient activation?
questions.feedback: Patient activation refers to a patient's knowledge, skills, and confidence to actively participate in their own health care decisions.

options.label: A
options.text: The process of registering for a health insurance plan
options.feedback: Plan registration is an enrollment activity, not activation in the patient engagement sense.

options.label: B
options.text: A patient's knowledge, skills, and confidence to manage their own health care
options.feedback: Patient activation refers to a patient's knowledge, skills, and confidence to actively participate in their own health care decisions.

options.label: C
options.text: The moment a patient's insurance coverage becomes effective
options.feedback: Coverage effective dates are administrative, not a measure of patient engagement.

options.label: D
options.text: A provider's decision to admit a patient to the hospital
options.feedback: Admission decisions are made by providers, not a reflection of patient activation.

questions.stem: Research shows that higher patient engagement is associated with which outcome?
questions.feedback: Studies show that higher patient activation and engagement is associated with better health outcomes and lower healthcare costs.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Engaged patients tend to manage conditions more effectively, not stay longer in hospitals.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Higher engagement is linked to lower costs, not higher costs.

options.label: C
options.text: Better health outcomes and lower costs
options.feedback: Studies show that higher patient activation and engagement is associated with better health outcomes and lower healthcare costs.

options.label: D
options.text: Fewer doctor visits and less preventive care
options.feedback: Engaged patients often use more preventive care, not less.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a domestic partner being a dependent?
questions.feedback: A domestic partner in a committed relationship can be listed as a dependent under the subscriber's health plan, depending on the plan's rules.

options.label: A
options.text: A coworker who shares an office with the subscriber
options.feedback: Coworkers do not qualify as dependents based on sharing a workspace.

options.label: B
options.text: A person in a committed relationship who is covered under the subscriber's health plan
options.feedback: A domestic partner in a committed relationship can be listed as a dependent under the subscriber's health plan, depending on the plan's rules.

options.label: C
options.text: A roommate who splits rent with the subscriber
options.feedback: Roommates are not considered dependents on a health plan.

options.label: D
options.text: A business partner who co-owns a company with the subscriber
options.feedback: A business partner is a professional relationship, not a dependent relationship.

questions.stem: Moving to a new state is considered what type of event for health insurance purposes?
questions.feedback: Moving to a new coverage area is a qualifying life event that triggers a Special Enrollment Period, allowing the person to enroll in or change a Marketplace plan.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Moving does not restart Open Enrollment; it triggers a Special Enrollment Period.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Premium adjustments are not directly triggered by moving.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Moving to a new coverage area is a qualifying life event that triggers a Special Enrollment Period, allowing the person to enroll in or change a Marketplace plan.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Moving does not automatically terminate coverage, though it may require a plan change.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about a health insurance premium is correct?
questions.feedback: A premium is paid — usually monthly — to maintain coverage, whether or not the patient uses any medical services.

options.label: A
options.text: The premium is only charged in months when the patient sees a doctor
options.feedback: Premiums are due every billing period regardless of utilization.

options.label: B
options.text: The premium counts toward the patient's deductible
options.feedback: Premiums do not count toward the deductible or out-of-pocket maximum.

options.label: C
options.text: The premium is paid regardless of whether the patient uses medical services
options.feedback: A premium is paid — usually monthly — to maintain coverage, whether or not the patient uses any medical services.

options.label: D
options.text: The premium is refunded at the end of the year if no claims are filed
options.feedback: Premiums are not refunded based on claims activity.

questions.stem: Medicare Part A primarily covers hospital stays.
questions.feedback: Medicare Part A is Hospital Insurance and covers inpatient hospital stays, skilled nursing facility care, hospice care, and some home health services.

questions.stem: Medicaid is a purely federal program with no state involvement in funding.
questions.feedback: Medicaid is jointly funded by the federal government and state governments. The federal share is determined by the Federal Medical Assistance Percentage (FMAP).

questions.stem: Commercial health insurance is the most common form of health coverage in the United States.
questions.feedback: Commercial (private) health insurance is indeed the most common form of health coverage in the United States.

questions.stem: Under HIPAA, health plans are classified as covered entities.
questions.feedback: Under HIPAA, health plans are one of the three categories of covered entities, alongside healthcare providers and clearinghouses.

questions.stem: Medicare Part B does not require enrollees to pay a monthly premium.
questions.feedback: Medicare Part B does require a monthly premium. For 2026, the standard Part B premium is $202.90 per month.

questions.stem: What is a payor in healthcare?
questions.feedback: A payor (also spelled payer) is an entity responsible for paying healthcare providers for services delivered to patients.

options.label: A
options.text: A patient who receives medical treatment
options.feedback: A patient receives care but is not necessarily the payor. The payor is the entity that pays the provider.

options.label: B
options.text: An entity responsible for paying healthcare providers for services delivered to patients
options.feedback: A payor (also spelled payer) is an entity responsible for paying healthcare providers for services delivered to patients.

options.label: C
options.text: A government agency that licenses doctors
options.feedback: A licensing agency regulates providers but does not pay for patient services.

options.label: D
options.text: A hospital that provides inpatient care
options.feedback: A hospital is a provider, not a payor. Providers deliver care; payors pay for it.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a government healthcare payor?
questions.feedback: Medicare is a federal health insurance program administered by CMS, making it a government payor.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Aetna is a commercial (private) health insurance company, not a government program.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicare is a federal health insurance program administered by CMS, making it a government payor.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Blue Cross Blue Shield is a commercial insurer, not a government program.

options.label: D
options.feedback: UnitedHealthcare is a commercial insurer, not a government program.

questions.stem: Which three broad categories do healthcare payors fall into?
questions.feedback: Healthcare payors fall into three broad categories: government programs (such as Medicare and Medicaid), commercial (private) insurance, and self-pay.

options.label: A
options.text: Government programs, commercial insurance, and self-pay
options.feedback: Healthcare payors fall into three broad categories: government programs (such as Medicare and Medicaid), commercial (private) insurance, and self-pay.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicare, Medicaid, and TRICARE are all government programs — they represent only one of the three categories.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Hospitals, clinics, and pharmacies are types of providers, not payors.

options.label: D
options.feedback: HMO, PPO, and EPO are types of commercial insurance plans, not the three broad payor categories.

questions.stem: Which federal agency administers the Medicare program?
questions.feedback: Medicare is administered by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), a federal agency within the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services.

options.label: A
options.feedback: The FDA regulates food and drugs, not health insurance programs.

options.label: B
options.text: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
options.feedback: The CDC focuses on disease prevention and public health, not health insurance administration.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Medicare is administered by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), a federal agency within the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The NIH conducts and supports biomedical research, not health insurance administration.

questions.stem: What does Medicare Part B cover?
questions.feedback: Medicare Part B (Medical Insurance) covers doctors' services, outpatient hospital care, durable medical equipment, and preventive services such as screenings and vaccinations.

options.label: A
options.text: Inpatient hospital stays and hospice care
options.feedback: Inpatient hospital stays and hospice care are covered by Medicare Part A, not Part B.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Prescription drug coverage is provided by Medicare Part D, not Part B.

options.label: C
options.text: Doctors' services, outpatient care, and preventive services
options.feedback: Medicare Part B (Medical Insurance) covers doctors' services, outpatient hospital care, durable medical equipment, and preventive services such as screenings and vaccinations.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Original Medicare (Parts A and B) generally does not cover routine dental and vision care.

questions.stem: What primarily determines Medicaid eligibility?
questions.feedback: Medicaid eligibility is based on income and household size rather than age, and people can apply at any time during the year.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Age is the primary factor for Medicare, not Medicaid. Medicaid eligibility is income-based.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicaid eligibility is based on income and household size rather than age, and people can apply at any time during the year.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Military service history determines eligibility for TRICARE or VA benefits, not Medicaid.

options.label: D
options.text: Number of years of U.S. citizenship
options.feedback: Citizenship duration is not the primary eligibility factor for Medicaid; income and household size are.

questions.stem: Which type of commercial health insurance plan typically requires a referral to see a specialist?
questions.feedback: An HMO (Health Maintenance Organization) usually requires you to choose a primary care doctor and get referrals to see specialists.

options.label: A
options.feedback: A PPO gives more flexibility to see any provider without a referral, though costs are lower when staying in-network.

options.label: B
options.feedback: An HMO (Health Maintenance Organization) usually requires you to choose a primary care doctor and get referrals to see specialists.

options.label: C
options.feedback: An EPO is a type of plan that limits coverage to in-network providers but does not typically require referrals.

options.label: D
options.feedback: A POS plan blends features of HMOs and PPOs but is not the plan type most commonly associated with specialist referrals.

questions.stem: What is a self-pay patient?
questions.feedback: A self-pay patient is someone who does not have health insurance or who chooses not to submit a claim to their insurance for a particular service.

options.label: A
options.text: A patient enrolled in Medicare Part A
options.feedback: A patient enrolled in Medicare Part A has government insurance coverage and is not self-pay.

options.label: B
options.text: A patient covered by an employer-sponsored group health plan
options.feedback: A patient with employer-sponsored insurance has commercial coverage and is not self-pay.

options.label: C
options.text: A patient who does not have health insurance or chooses not to file a claim with their insurer
options.feedback: A self-pay patient is someone who does not have health insurance or who chooses not to submit a claim to their insurance for a particular service.

options.label: D
options.text: A patient whose payor has denied their claim
options.feedback: A claim denial does not automatically make a patient self-pay; the patient may appeal or the provider may resubmit.

questions.stem: What is the most common way Americans obtain private health insurance?
questions.feedback: Employer-sponsored group health plans are the most common way Americans obtain private health insurance.

options.label: A
options.text: Purchasing directly from an insurance company
options.feedback: Direct purchase is an option but is less common than employer-sponsored coverage.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Employer-sponsored group health plans are the most common way Americans obtain private health insurance.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The Marketplace is an option for those without employer coverage but is not the most common source.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Medicaid is a government program, not private insurance.

questions.stem: Which type of health plan is exempt from HIPAA covered-entity requirements?
questions.feedback: A group health plan with fewer than 50 participants that is administered solely by the employer is exempt from HIPAA covered-entity requirements.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicare is a government program and is a HIPAA-covered health plan.

options.label: B
options.text: A group health plan with fewer than 50 participants administered solely by the employer
options.feedback: A group health plan with fewer than 50 participants that is administered solely by the employer is exempt from HIPAA covered-entity requirements.

options.label: C
options.feedback: HMOs are health plans and are generally HIPAA-covered entities.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Medicaid is a government program and is a HIPAA-covered health plan.

questions.stem: Original Medicare consists of two parts: Part A (Hospital Insurance) and Part B (Medical Insurance).
questions.feedback: Original Medicare is made up of Part A, which covers hospital and inpatient services, and Part B, which covers outpatient and physician services.

questions.stem: Most people pay no monthly premium for Medicare Part A because they or their spouse paid Medicare taxes for at least 10 years.
questions.feedback: If a person or their spouse paid Medicare taxes for 40 or more quarters (10 years), they qualify for premium-free Part A.

questions.stem: Medicare is only available to people aged 65 and older.
questions.feedback: While Medicare primarily serves people 65 and older, it is also available to certain people under 65 who have disabilities, End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD), or ALS.

questions.stem: People already receiving Social Security benefits are automatically enrolled in Medicare Part A and Part B when they become eligible.
questions.feedback: Individuals who are already receiving Social Security benefits are automatically enrolled in both Part A and Part B when they reach Medicare eligibility.

questions.stem: Medicaid eligibility rules and covered services are identical in every state.
questions.feedback: Medicaid is administered by individual states according to federal requirements, so eligibility rules and covered services can vary from state to state.

questions.stem: Medicaid covers eligible low-income adults, children, pregnant women, elderly adults, and people with disabilities.
questions.feedback: Medicaid is a joint federal and state program that provides health coverage to these eligible populations.

questions.stem: The Federal Medical Assistance Percentage (FMAP) is the same for every state.
questions.feedback: The FMAP varies by state based on per capita income. States with lower per capita incomes receive a higher federal share of Medicaid costs.

questions.stem: Unlike Medicare, Medicaid allows people to apply at any time during the year rather than only during open enrollment periods.
questions.feedback: Medicaid does not have a limited enrollment period — eligible individuals can apply at any time during the year.

questions.stem: An EPO (Exclusive Provider Organization) plan typically covers out-of-network care for non-emergency situations.
questions.feedback: An EPO limits coverage to in-network providers, meaning services from out-of-network providers are generally not covered except in emergencies.

questions.stem: Employer-sponsored group health plans typically share the cost of premiums between the employer and employees.
questions.feedback: In employer-sponsored plans, the employer typically shares the cost of premiums with employees, making coverage more affordable.

questions.stem: An uninsured individual is someone who is not enrolled in any group health plan, individual health insurance coverage, or federal health care program.
questions.feedback: This is the definition of an uninsured individual — a person without any form of health insurance coverage.

questions.stem: Under the No Surprises Act, providers are only required to give a good faith estimate if the patient specifically requests one.
questions.feedback: Under the No Surprises Act, providers must give uninsured or self-pay patients a good faith estimate of expected charges before or upon scheduling a service, regardless of whether the patient requests one.

questions.stem: HIPAA-covered health plans include health insurance companies, HMOs, and government programs such as Medicare and Medicaid.
questions.feedback: All of these are classified as health plans under HIPAA and must comply with the Privacy Rule and Security Rule.

questions.stem: A PPO plan requires members to choose a primary care physician before they can receive any covered services.
questions.feedback: A PPO (Preferred Provider Organization) generally does not require members to choose a primary care physician or get referrals to see specialists. PPOs offer more flexibility in choosing providers.

questions.stem: When a patient has coverage from more than one payor, the payor that pays first is called the primary payer.
questions.feedback: Coordination of benefits rules determine which payor pays first, and that payor is referred to as the primary payer.

questions.stem: Medicare Part B covers prescription drug costs.
questions.feedback: Prescription drug coverage is provided by Medicare Part D, not Part B. Part B covers doctors' services, outpatient care, and preventive services.

questions.stem: Aetna, Blue Cross Blue Shield, and UnitedHealthcare are examples of government healthcare programs.
questions.feedback: Aetna, Blue Cross Blue Shield, and UnitedHealthcare are major commercial (private) health insurance companies, not government programs.

questions.stem: Commercial health insurance can be obtained through an employer, purchased on the Health Insurance Marketplace, or bought directly from an insurance company.
questions.feedback: These are the three main channels through which individuals can obtain commercial health insurance coverage.

questions.stem: Medicare Part A covers durable medical equipment such as wheelchairs and oxygen tanks.
questions.feedback: Durable medical equipment is covered under Medicare Part B, not Part A. Part A covers inpatient hospital stays, skilled nursing facility care, hospice care, and some home health services.

questions.stem: Which of the following people would be eligible for Medicare under age 65?
questions.feedback: Medicare is available to certain people under 65 who have disabilities, including those who have received Social Security Disability Insurance for 24 months.

questions.stem: Who administers the Medicaid provider enrollment process?

questions.stem: Which of the following triggers a healthcare provider's status as a HIPAA-covered entity?
